notes: Human-reviewed recipe source audit. Use this to separate culinary/source verification from static route eligibility.

kedjenou-ci.official_source_status: No direct Côte d'Ivoire ministry or tourism recipe page was found in web search on 2026-04-27. Treat the named cookbook-derived, encyclopedia, and culinary references below as the current evidence set until an official source is located.
kedjenou-ci.review_summary: The AfroKitchen Kedjenou entry matches the core cross-source pattern: Ivorian/Baoulé or Akan dish, chicken or guinea fowl, sealed low-heat cooking in a canari or tightly sealed pot, little or no added liquid, tomatoes/onions/chile/aromatics, and service with attiéké or rice.
recommended_changes: Keep the no-added-water positioning. Multiple references describe little or no added liquid, while a few modern adaptations use a small amount of water or stock.
Add salt and pepper to the ingredient list or visibly mark them as pantry seasoning because the method already tells cooks to season with salt and pepper.
Do not make bell pepper or green onion required. They appear in some modern recipes, but the current tomato, onion, eggplant, garlic, ginger, chile, and bay profile is defensible.
Keep the guinea fowl substitution. Multiple references describe chicken or guinea hen/fowl as traditional.

chakhchoukha-dz.official_source_status: No Algerian ministry or tourism recipe page was found in the first web pass. Current evidence combines culinary references, an encyclopedia entry, and cookbook/community sources.
chakhchoukha-dz.review_summary: The AfroKitchen Chakhchoukha entry matches the core pattern: Algerian torn rougag or thin flatbread served with a tomato-based meat stew, chickpeas, onions, ras el hanout, chile heat, and a communal platter. The main practical gap was that the method used oil, stew seasoning, and water that were not visible in the ingredient list.
recommended_changes: Keep the rougag plus lamb stew structure.
Add cooking oil, stew salt, black pepper, and water as practical ingredients because the method already relies on them.
Consider a later regional variation note for cumin, caraway, garlic, and vegetables because sources vary by region.

couscous-royal-dz.official_source_status: UNESCO confirms couscous knowledge and consumption practices as shared intangible heritage of Algeria, Mauritania, Morocco, and Tunisia. Recipe-level official guidance for this specific royal version was not found in the first web pass.
couscous-royal-dz.review_summary: The recipe is directionally sound as an Algerian celebratory couscous page, but 'royal' is a flexible restaurant/diaspora style rather than a single fixed official formula. The page needed visible ingredients for onion, oil, water, salt, pepper, ras el hanout, and harissa because the cooking steps already use them.
recommended_changes: Keep the couscoussier steaming method and lamb, merguez, chickpea, turnip, carrot, courgette structure.
Add the broth aromatics and pantry ingredients that already appear in the method.
Use softer copy for the royal-style framing because sources treat couscous as a broad Maghrebi heritage with many local versions.

recipe.description: A celebratory Algerian-style couscous with fluffy steamed semolina, lamb, merguez sausage, chickpeas, turnips, carrots, courgettes, and a fragrant broth.
recipe.story: Couscous is inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list as shared heritage of Algeria, Mauritania, Morocco, and Tunisia. In Algeria, couscous is a family anchor, especially around Friday lunches and celebrations. This royal-style version brings together meat, merguez, vegetables, chickpeas, and broth for a generous feast rather than a single fixed national formula.

makroud-dz.official_source_status: No Algerian ministry or tourism recipe page was found in the first web pass. Current evidence combines recipe references and broader Maghrebi culinary summaries.
makroud-dz.review_summary: The Makroud entry matches the main pattern: diamond semolina pastry, date filling with cinnamon and orange blossom water, frying, then honey coating. The practical gap was that the method used salt and warm water, while the ingredient list did not show them.
recommended_changes: Keep the date, cinnamon, orange blossom water, fried semolina, and honey structure.
Add salt and warm water to the dough ingredients because the method already requires them.
Rename the semolina to coarse or medium semolina in the static page because many references use coarse semolina or a coarse/fine blend for texture.

recipe.story: Makroud is loved across the Maghreb and is part of Algerian patisserie for Eid, weddings, and guest tables. Algerian versions commonly wrap spiced date paste in semolina dough, shape it into diamonds, fry or bake it, then finish it with warm honey or syrup scented with orange blossom water.

amiwo-bj.official_source_status: A recipe contributed by the EU Delegation to Benin was found in the Africa-Europe Cook Book. No Benin ministry recipe page was found in the first web pass.
amiwo-bj.review_summary: The Amiwo entry matches the core Beninese pattern: cornmeal or corn flour cooked into red tomato-based paste, served with chicken or fish and sauce. The page needed garlic, water, salt, pepper, and a palm-oil/vegetable-oil wording because these appear across sources and in the cooking method.
recommended_changes: Keep cornmeal, tomato, onion, oil, and grilled or fried protein pairing.
Add water, garlic, salt, and black pepper so the ingredient list matches the cooking method and common source patterns.
Allow vegetable oil as a practical fallback, but mention palm oil because several sources treat it as the traditional flavor base.

recipe.story: Amiwo, also called red paste, is a Beninese cornmeal dish cooked with tomato, onion, oil, and seasoning until it sets into a savory mound. It is commonly served for family meals and celebrations with chicken, fish, or a tomato-pepper sauce.

fields.instruction: Heat oil, fry onion and garlic until soft. Add tomato paste and cook 3 minutes. Add blended tomatoes, salt, and black pepper, then cook until reduced and thick, about 15 minutes.

wagasi-grille-bj.review_summary: The Wagasi page is directionally sound: wagasi is a West African cow-milk cheese strongly associated with northern Benin and Parakou, commonly fried, boiled, or cooked in sauce, and often eaten with pepper. The practical gap was the piment sauce using lime and salt without listing them.
recommended_changes: Keep wagasi with oil and pepper sauce.
Add lime and salt because the piment sauce method uses them.
Keep paneer or halloumi as substitutions only for readers outside West Africa, not as cultural equivalents.

kuli-kuli-bj.official_source_status: No Benin official recipe page was found in the first web pass. Evidence comes from regional summaries and recipe references for a snack eaten across West Africa, including Benin.
kuli-kuli-bj.review_summary: The Kuli-Kuli entry matches the common West African method: roasted peanuts are ground into paste, excess oil is extracted, the paste is seasoned, shaped, and fried until crisp. No static recipe changes were needed in this pass.
recommended_changes: Keep the oil-extraction step as essential.
Keep shapes flexible, since sticks, rings, rounds, and balls all appear in source patterns.
Keep Benin framing as part of a broader West African snack tradition rather than exclusive origin language.

morogo-bw.official_source_status: No Botswana government recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence combines Botswana travel/culture references, Slow Food Ark of Taste, and local recipe writing.
morogo-bw.review_summary: The AfroKitchen Morogo page is directionally sound: morogo is a Botswana and Southern African leafy-green dish, often using wild spinach, cowpea leaves, amaranth, or pumpkin leaves, cooked with onion, tomato, oil, salt, and sometimes peanuts or peanut butter. The current peanut powder version is defensible as a richer morogo wa dinawa-style variation.
recommended_changes: Keep leafy greens with tomato, onion, oil, salt, and peanut powder.
Keep the serving suggestion with pap, bogobe, or seswaa.
In a later variation note, distinguish plain morogo from morogo wa dinawa/cowpea-leaf versions.

seswaa-bw.official_source_status: No Botswana government recipe page was found in this pass. Botswana travel/culture references and recipe sources consistently identify seswaa as the national dish.
seswaa-bw.review_summary: The Seswaa page matches the cross-source pattern: beef or goat boiled until tender, shredded or pounded, seasoned very simply with salt and sometimes onion, pepper, or bay in modern home adaptations, then served with pap/bogobe and morogo.
recommended_changes: Keep beef, water, salt, long simmering, pounding/shredding, and bogobe or pap service.
Soften the copy so it does not imply that every version uses only meat, water, and salt while the recipe itself includes onion and optional pepper.
Keep bay leaves out of the core recipe for now because they appear in adapted versions rather than the most stripped-down traditional framing.

recipe.description: Botswana's national dish: beef slow-boiled until falling apart, then shredded and pounded to a tender pulled-meat texture with simple salt-forward seasoning.
recipe.story: Seswaa is the pride of Botswana, served at weddings, funerals, national celebrations, and important family gatherings. The traditional heart of the dish is restrained: meat, water, salt, and patient cooking, with some home versions adding onion or pepper. The simplicity lets Botswana's beef or goat shine.

vetkoek-bw.review_summary: The Vetkoek with Mince page matches the Southern African pattern: yeast dough fried into golden rounds, split and filled with savory mince. The main fix was practical: the mince step used salt, pepper, and curry powder, but those seasonings were not visible in the ingredient list.
recommended_changes: Keep magwinya/vetkoek as a Botswana and broader Southern African street-food framing.
Add curry powder, salt, and black pepper to the mince filling ingredients.
Keep sweet fillings as a note, not the default, because this route is specifically the mince version.

babenda-bf.official_source_status: FAO indexes Babenda from a Burkina Faso local-products cookbook, and a Burkina recipe PDF gives a detailed ingredient and preparation pattern. No tourism-ministry recipe page was found in this pass.
babenda-bf.review_summary: The prior Babenda page had the right broad idea of greens and soumbala, but the grain base was off. Stronger Burkina sources describe modern Babenda as sorrel and African spinach or amaranth leaves cooked with rice, pounded peanuts, soumbala, dried fish, pepper, salt, and shea butter or oil for serving.
recommended_changes: Replace millet-flour porridge framing with rice, leafy greens, and pounded peanuts.
Make soumbala a small seasoning rather than the main body of the dish.
Add dried fish, pepper, salt, and water so the method matches the source pattern.

recipe.description: A nourishing Burkina Faso pot of leafy greens, rice, pounded peanuts, soumbala, and dried fish, finished with shea butter, pepper, and salt.
recipe.story: Babenda is a Mossi-rooted Burkinabe dish born from resilience during difficult harvest years, when families stretched edible leaves with a little grain and water. Modern versions are richer, often using sorrel leaves, African spinach or amaranth, rice, pounded peanuts, soumbala, and dried fish, then shared hot with shea butter or oil.

fields.name: leafy greens
fields.prep_note: sorrel leaves and African spinach, amaranth, or spinach, chopped

fields.title: Cook the greens
fields.instruction: Bring a pot of water to a boil. Add the chopped African spinach or amaranth first, then add sorrel leaves once the greens begin to soften. Simmer for about 15 minutes.
fields.tip: Sorrel brings tang, while amaranth or spinach gives the dish body.

fields.title: Add rice and peanuts
fields.instruction: Add the rinsed rice and pounded peanuts to the greens. Stir well so the peanuts disperse through the pot.

fields.title: Season the pot
fields.instruction: Add soumbala and dried fish powder, then stir carefully with a wooden spoon so the mixture does not stick.

fields.title: Simmer slowly
fields.instruction: Cover and cook over low heat for 25 to 30 minutes, stirring often, until the rice is tender and the greens and peanuts form a thick, hearty mixture.
Serve hot with shea butter or oil, pepper, and salt at the table. Babenda can also sit beside toh for a fuller meal.

poulet-bicyclette-bf.official_source_status: No Burkina Faso ministry recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence combines Burkina media/culture references and culinary recipe sources.
poulet-bicyclette-bf.review_summary: The Poulet Bicyclette page is directionally sound: local free-range chicken grilled over charcoal with a sharp marinade of garlic, mustard or vinaigrette, citrus or vinegar, oil, chile, herbs, salt, and pepper. The main practical gap was missing salt, pepper, and herbs in the ingredient list, plus copy that over-explained the bicycle name.
recommended_changes: Keep free-range chicken and charcoal-grilled maquis/street-food framing.
Add salt, black pepper, and parsley to match common marinade patterns.
Soften the etymology: the name is tied to local chickens and market/street context, not one single origin story.

recipe.description: Grilled local free-range chicken marinated in mustard, citrus, garlic, chile, oil, and herbs, then cooked over charcoal until smoky and crisp.
recipe.story: Poulet bicyclette is the beloved local chicken of Burkina Faso's maquis and street grills. The name is tied to small, firm, free-range birds and the market life around them, including vendors transporting chickens by bicycle or motorbike. The dish is simple, smoky, and best eaten hot with onions, fries, rice, attieke, or a sharp pepper sauce.

fields.instruction: Mix mustard, lime juice, garlic, parsley, cayenne, oil, salt, and pepper into a paste.

riz-gras-bf.official_source_status: No Burkina Faso ministry recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence combines culinary references, food-history writing, and encyclopedic summaries.
riz-gras-bf.review_summary: The Riz Gras page is directionally sound as a Burkina-style one-pot tomato rice with meat, oil, onion, tomato paste, vegetables, and rice. The practical gaps were garlic, water or stock, salt, pepper, and chile, all common in source patterns or used by the method.
recommended_changes: Keep rice cooked directly in the seasoned meat-tomato broth.
Add garlic, cooking water or stock, salt, pepper, and optional hot pepper.
Avoid calling every spice mandatory because regional and household versions vary widely.

fields.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot. Season meat with salt and black pepper, then brown on all sides. Remove and reserve.

Fry onions and garlic in the same pot until golden. Add tomato paste and cook 3 minutes. Add 5 cups water or stock, optional hot pepper, and the browned meat. Bring to a boil and simmer until meat is tender, about 25 minutes.

burundian-isombe.official_source_status: No Burundi government recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence combines Burundi travel/tour references, a children/culture PDF, and recipe references.
burundian-isombe.review_summary: The Isombe page matches the core pattern of cassava or manioc leaves simmered with aromatics and palm oil, often served with ubugari. The main gap was that many Burundi/Rwanda/Great Lakes recipes include peanut butter or pounded peanuts, and the page did not make that component visible.
recommended_changes: Keep cassava leaves, palm oil, onion, garlic, tomato, eggplant, water, salt, and optional dried fish.
Add peanut butter or pounded peanuts and optional bell pepper/spinach variation.
Keep fish optional because vegetarian and fish/meat versions both appear.

fields.instruction: Bring to a boil, then reduce heat. Stir in peanut butter or pounded peanuts. Cover and simmer, stirring occasionally, until leaves are very tender and the sauce is thick.
fields.tip: Burundian cooks simmer isombe patiently so the cassava leaves soften and the peanut or palm-oil richness rounds out the sauce.

Add parsley as an optional herb and include it in the marinade instruction.
Fill the empty serving pairing with ubugari, fried plantains, tomato-onion salad, or rice.

koki-beans-cm.official_source_status: No Cameroon ministry recipe page was found in this pass, but the national intangible-heritage context is referenced in secondary sources. Evidence agrees on black-eyed peas, palm oil, chile, leaf wrapping, and steaming.
koki-beans-cm.review_summary: The Koki Beans page is sound on technique and ingredients. The only change is copy: sources place koki across Littoral, South-West, and West Cameroon rather than only a single Bamileke grasslands frame.
recommended_changes: Keep black-eyed peas, palm oil, chile, banana leaves, water, and steaming.

Keep plantains as a serving pairing.

recipe.story: Koki is a golden Cameroonian bean cake associated with communities across the Littoral, South-West, and West regions. Black-eyed peas or cowpeas are soaked, peeled, crushed into a batter, enriched with red palm oil, wrapped in banana leaves, and steamed until set. The result is dense, fragrant, and deeply comforting.

pastel-diablo-cv.official_source_status: No Cabo Verde government recipe page was found in this pass. Portuguese-language recipe archives and Cape Verdean food writers agree on the tuna, chile, sweet-potato, and cornmeal pattern.
pastel-diablo-cv.review_summary: The previous Pastel com Diabo Dentro page had the right tuna/chile idea but the dough was too generic. Stronger Cape Verdean sources use sweet potato plus cornmeal for the dough, with a spicy tuna filling seasoned with onion, tomato, garlic, chile, vinegar or lemon, and salt.
recommended_changes: Replace all-purpose flour dough with sweet potato and cornmeal dough.
Add garlic, vinegar or lime, and salt to the tuna filling.
Keep tuna and malagueta as the default filling; beef/pork versions exist but are less aligned with the stronger Cape Verdean sources found here.

recipe.description: Cape Verdean fried sweet-potato and cornmeal pastries stuffed with a fiery tuna, tomato, onion, garlic, and malagueta filling.
recipe.story: These fiery little pastries are a staple of Cape Verdean snacks and celebrations. The devil inside is the sharp heat of malagueta and the savory tuna filling tucked into a sweet-potato and cornmeal dough before frying.

fields.title: Make the dough
fields.instruction: Boil sweet potatoes until tender, then peel and mash smooth. Mix in cornmeal gradually, adding a little sweet potato cooking water as needed, until the dough is moldable and does not crack. Rest covered for 30 minutes.
fields.tip: The dough should be soft enough to shape but firm enough to hold the filling.

fields.instruction: Fry onion until soft. Add garlic, tomatoes, and malagueta peppers, then cook 5 minutes. Add tuna, vinegar or lime, and salt, and cook 5 more minutes until dry. Season well.
fields.tip: The filling must be dry because wet filling makes soggy pastels.

gozo-cf.official_source_status: No Central African Republic government recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence comes from country food references and encyclopedic cuisine summaries.
gozo-cf.review_summary: The Gozo page is sound: cassava flour and water cooked into a stiff paste used with sauces and stews. No static recipe changes were needed.
recommended_changes: Keep cassava flour, water, optional salt, vigorous stirring, and sauce pairing.
Keep peanut, okra, meat, fish, or vegetable sauces as pairings.
Do not over-specify one sauce as mandatory.

kanda-ti-nyma-cf.official_source_status: No Central African Republic government recipe page was found in this pass. Evidence combines culinary references for kanda and kanda ti nyma variants.
kanda-ti-nyma-cf.review_summary: The previous Kanda ti Nyma page had a peanut sauce, but missed the okra that distinguishes the kanda ti nyma pattern in multiple sources. It also used ground peanuts inside the meatballs, while sources more often bind the meatballs with egg and season them with garlic and chile, reserving peanuts for the sauce.
recommended_changes: Move away from peanut-bound meatballs and use egg, garlic, and chile in the meatball mix.
Add okra to the sauce as a defining ingredient.
Keep peanut butter sauce and gozo/rice/plantain serving options.

recipe.story: Kanda ti nyma means meat balls in Sango. In this researched version, the beef meatballs are seasoned with onion, garlic, chile, salt, and egg, then simmered in a peanut and okra sauce until tender. Serve it with gozo, rice, or plantain for a hearty Central African Republic table.

fields.instruction: Combine beef mince, beaten eggs, finely diced onion, garlic, chile, and salt in a bowl. Mix well with your hands until the mixture holds together. Shape into golf ball-sized meatballs.
fields.tip: The eggs help the meatballs hold while they simmer in the peanut-okra sauce.

fields.instruction: In the same pan, saute the diced onion until soft. Add okra and cook 5 minutes. Stir in the peanut butter and 1 cup of warm water, mixing until smooth.

sauce-graine-cf.official_source_status: No Central African Republic government recipe page was found in this pass, and direct CAR-specific sauce graine recipe evidence was weaker than for other dishes. Evidence supports palm fruit sauce broadly across West and Central Africa, but the CAR-specific framing should stay conservative.
sauce-graine-cf.review_summary: The Sauce Graine page is directionally sound as a palm-fruit sauce served with starch, fish, meat, and aromatics, but source coverage was not strongly CAR-specific. The practical gaps were palm oil for the saut? step and salt for seasoning.
recommended_changes: Keep palm fruit pulp, smoked fish, tomato, onion, chile, water, and gozo/rice pairing.
Add palm oil and salt because the method uses them.
Keep confidence low until a stronger CAR-specific source is located.

moi-moi-ng.review_summary: The AfroKitchen version follows the common Nigerian pattern: peeled black-eyed or brown beans, pepper, onion, oil, seasoning, optional crayfish or egg, and steaming in leaves, tins, ramekins, or foil until set.
recommended_changes: Keep boiled egg and crayfish optional because sources treat them as common add-ins, not mandatory.
Do not over-thicken the batter. Multiple references rely on enough water to steam into a soft pudding.

efo-riro-ng.official_source_status: No official Nigerian government recipe page was found in this batch pass. Sources include regional food references, encyclopedia context, and diaspora reporting.
efo-riro-ng.review_summary: The common references agree on leafy greens, pepper blend, palm oil, locust beans or iru, crayfish, onion, and assorted meat or fish. Spinach is a practical diaspora substitute for Nigerian greens.
recommended_changes: Keep iru and crayfish visible because they define the classic Yoruba flavor.
Use spinach as an available substitute but name amaranth or efo shoko as the traditional green.

pepper-soup-ng.official_source_status: No official Nigerian government recipe page was found in this batch pass. The reference set is regional food writing and encyclopedia context.
pepper-soup-ng.review_summary: The recipe follows the common Nigerian pepper soup format: meat or fish simmered with onion, pepper, ehu or pepper soup spice, scent leaf or uziza, and enough broth to serve hot.
recommended_changes: Keep protein flexible because sources show goat, chicken, catfish, cow foot, and assorted meat variants.
Name ehu or calabash nutmeg because several Nigerian sources identify it as a defining spice.

puff-puff-ng.official_source_status: No official Nigerian government recipe page was found in this batch pass. The references are culinary sources and encyclopedia context.
puff-puff-ng.review_summary: The recipe follows the widely repeated puff puff profile: flour, yeast, sugar, salt, warm water, optional nutmeg, one rise, then deep frying until golden.

waakye-gh.official_source_status: A Ghana Revenue Authority newsletter result mentions waakye, but this batch did not find an official recipe standard. The dish is verified through food references and sorghum industry documentation.
waakye-gh.review_summary: The core profile is stable across sources: rice and beans cooked together with sorghum or millet leaves for color, then served with shito, stew, eggs, plantain, fish, meat, salad, or spaghetti.
recommended_changes: Keep sorghum or millet leaves as the preferred ingredient and baking soda as fallback.
Describe accompaniments as optional because the rice-and-beans base is the recipe.

red-red-gh.official_source_status: No official Ghanaian recipe standard was found in this batch pass. The dish is confirmed by culinary references and encyclopedia context.

githeri-ke.official_source_status: Kenya's Recipe Book of Common Mixed Dishes includes githeri. Additional accessible references confirm the maize-and-beans core.
githeri-ke.review_summary: The core dish is maize and beans boiled together. A light onion-tomato finish is a common modern approach, while the simple boiled version remains valid.
recommended_changes: Keep maize and beans as the non-negotiable core.
Mark tomatoes, carrots, and spices as a modern finish rather than the base identity.

mukimo-ke.official_source_status: Kenya's Recipe Book of Common Mixed Dishes includes mukimo. Additional Kenyan and humanitarian sources support the same ingredient pattern.
mukimo-ke.review_summary: The common pattern is potatoes mashed with maize, peas or beans, and greens. Onion or butter is optional, while pumpkin leaves or spinach provide the green color.
recommended_changes: Keep pumpkin leaves as traditional and spinach as an accessible substitute.
Do not over-blend. Mukimo can be smooth but should still feel like a mash.

malva-pudding-za.official_source_status: No official South African government recipe standard was found in this batch pass. The recipe is supported by encyclopedia, food media, and hospitality recipe references.
malva-pudding-za.review_summary: Sources consistently identify the South African dessert as an apricot-jam sponge leavened with bicarbonate and vinegar, then soaked with warm butter, sugar, and cream or milk sauce.
recommended_changes: Keep apricot jam, vinegar, and bicarbonate because they show up as defining ingredients.
Pour the sauce over the pudding while hot so it absorbs properly.

sources.title: Salty Ginger - Traditional South African Malva Pudding

boule-td.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Regional cuisine references agree on millet or sorghum flour cooked with water into a firm staple.
boule-td.review_summary: Boule is sound as a Chadian millet or sorghum flour staple cooked into a firm paste for sauces. The main improvement is presentation copy and clearer step wording.
recommended_changes: Keep the millet or sorghum base.
Keep salt optional and keep the method focused on lump-free stirring.

recipe.description: A smooth, firm millet or sorghum porridge shaped for scooping up Chadian sauces and stews.
recipe.story: Boule is the quiet anchor of many Chadian meals. Millet or sorghum flour is worked into boiling water until it turns smooth, firm, and elastic enough to tear by hand and scoop through stews.
recipe.regional_variations: Millet flour is common, but sorghum or maize flour may be used depending on the household and region.

replace_steps.title: Start the slurry
replace_steps.instruction: Bring most of the water to a boil in a heavy pot. In a bowl, mix 1 cup of millet flour with 1 cup cold water until smooth.
replace_steps.tip: A cold slurry helps stop lumps before they start.

replace_steps.title: Cook the base
replace_steps.instruction: Pour the slurry into the boiling water while stirring. Cook until it thickens and turns glossy.

replace_steps.title: Beat in the flour
replace_steps.instruction: Add the remaining flour gradually, stirring hard with a strong wooden spoon until the mixture becomes stiff.
replace_steps.tip: This is arm work. The smoother the stirring, the better the boule.

replace_steps.title: Finish the boule
replace_steps.instruction: Keep stirring over low heat until the boule pulls away from the pot and tastes fully cooked.

replace_steps.title: Shape and serve
replace_steps.instruction: Wet a bowl or spoon, shape the boule into rounds, and serve hot with stew or sauce.

sources.title: Sunheron - What to Eat in Chad

daraba-td.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Multiple Chadian recipe references agree on okra, greens, peanut butter, tomatoes, and starchy vegetables.
daraba-td.review_summary: Daraba needed the full vegetable body made visible. Stronger Chadian-style references use okra, greens, tomato, peanut butter, and often sweet potato or eggplant with water or stock.
recommended_changes: Add water or stock, salt, pepper, sweet potato, and optional eggplant.
Cook firm vegetables before adding greens so the stew is creamy but not overcooked.

recipe.description: A Chadian peanut-okra stew with greens, tomato, sweet potato, and a creamy sauce made for boule or rice.
recipe.story: Daraba is the kind of stew that feeds a table without fuss. Okra gives it body, peanut butter gives it richness, and greens turn the pot earthy and generous.
recipe.regional_variations: Sweet potato, pumpkin, or eggplant can move in and out of the pot depending on the market.

replace_ingredients.name: mixed greens
replace_ingredients.prep_note: spinach, sorrel, cassava leaves, or sweet potato leaves, chopped

replace_steps.title: Start the base
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot. Cook onion until soft, then add tomatoes and scotch bonnet if using. Cook until the tomatoes collapse.

replace_steps.title: Simmer the firm vegetables
replace_steps.instruction: Add sweet potato, eggplant if using, okra, and water or stock. Simmer until the sweet potato is almost tender.

replace_steps.title: Stir in the peanut
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk peanut butter with a ladle of hot broth, then stir it back into the pot until the sauce turns creamy.
replace_steps.tip: Loosening the peanut butter first keeps the sauce smooth.

replace_steps.title: Add the greens
replace_steps.instruction: Fold in the chopped greens, cover, and cook until tender. The stew should be thick enough to sit on boule.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Season with salt and black pepper. Serve hot with boule, rice, or sorghum.

sources.title: Tastes From The Road - Daraba from Chad

la-bouillie-td.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Chadian references support la bouillie as millet, flour, rice, milk, sugar, lemon, or peanut-enriched breakfast porridge.
la-bouillie-td.review_summary: The millet porridge structure is sound. It needed salt and softer presentation language, with milk kept as a finishing choice rather than the whole cooking liquid.
recommended_changes: Add a pinch of salt.
Keep the porridge adjustable, from spoonable to drinkable.

recipe.description: A warm Chadian millet porridge sweetened with sugar and finished with milk, vanilla, and lemon zest.
recipe.story: La Bouillie is morning comfort in a bowl. The millet slurry cooks into a smooth porridge, then milk, sugar, vanilla, and lemon make it gentle enough for breakfast or a light evening meal.

sources.title: Africa Inland Mission - Make Chad Porridge PDF
The Afrikan Store - Chad's Porridge Recipe

langouste-vanille.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Strong culinary references identify langouste a la vanille as the Comoros national dish.
langouste-vanille.review_summary: The Comorian lobster and vanilla idea is right, but the sauce should be lighter and more elegant. Cream is common, while coconut cream is a reasonable island-style option.
recommended_changes: Reduce the cream volume.
Keep vanilla subtle and let the lobster stay central.

recipe.best_served_with: Steamed rice, sauteed greens, or pilao ya nazi
recipe.description: Comorian lobster finished in a delicate vanilla cream sauce, elegant without hiding the sweetness of the seafood.
recipe.story: Langouste a la Vanille works because the vanilla is gentle. It perfumes the cream, softens the wine and shallots, and lets the lobster taste like the sea.

replace_ingredients.name: lobster tails
replace_ingredients.substitution: small lobster, langoustines, shrimp, or scallops

replace_steps.title: Sear the lobster
replace_steps.instruction: Brush lobster with oil and sear flesh-side down until lightly golden. Remove before it is fully cooked.

replace_steps.title: Start the sauce
replace_steps.instruction: Melt butter in the pan. Cook shallots until soft, then add white wine and reduce by half.

replace_steps.title: Infuse the vanilla
replace_steps.instruction: Add vanilla seeds, pods, and cream. Simmer gently until the sauce lightly coats a spoon.

replace_steps.title: Finish the lobster
replace_steps.instruction: Return lobster to the pan and cook gently until opaque and just firm.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Remove vanilla pods, season with salt and white pepper, and add a little lime if the sauce needs brightness.

sources.title: Saveur - Grilled Lobsters With Vanilla Cream Sauce
International Cuisine - Comoros Lobster with Vanilla Sauce

mkatra-foutra.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Multiple Comoros-focused culinary references agree on coconut milk, yeast, flour, sesame, and skillet flatbread style.
mkatra-foutra.review_summary: The old page treated Mkatra Foutra like a baked loaf. Stronger Comorian references describe it as a yeast coconut flatbread cooked on a pan and finished with sesame.
recommended_changes: Change the method from baked loaf to pan-cooked coconut flatbread.
Remove eggs, butter glaze, and loaf shaping from the public recipe.

recipe.description: Comorian coconut flatbread made with yeast, coconut milk, and sesame, cooked on a hot pan until soft and golden.
recipe.story: Mkatra Foutra should feel like bread from the skillet, not cake from the oven. Coconut milk makes the dough tender, yeast gives it lift, and sesame seeds toast against the pan.
recipe.best_served_with: Tea, honey, butter, fish curry, rougaille, or coconut-based stews
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks make smaller rounds, others cook one larger flatbread and cut it into wedges.

replace_steps.title: Mix the dough
replace_steps.instruction: Mix flour, yeast, sugar, and salt. Add warm coconut milk and knead until soft and smooth.

replace_steps.title: Let it rise
replace_steps.instruction: Cover and leave in a warm place until puffy and roughly doubled.

replace_steps.title: Shape the breads
replace_steps.instruction: Divide into 8 pieces. Flatten each into a round and press sesame seeds into one side.

replace_steps.title: Cook on the pan
replace_steps.instruction: Lightly oil a heavy pan and cook each round over medium heat until golden on both sides and cooked through.

replace_steps.title: Serve warm
replace_steps.instruction: Serve warm with tea, honey, butter, fish curry, or a coconut relish.

sources.title: Eat This NY - Mkatra Foutra from Comoros

pilao-nazi.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Comorian cuisine references support coconut-heavy rice and pilao as common island accompaniments.
pilao-nazi.review_summary: Pilao ya Nazi is sound as a Comorian coconut rice pilaf. It mostly needed serving context and calmer copy.
recommended_changes: Keep coconut milk and warm spices.
Add clear serving pairings.

recipe.best_served_with: Langouste a la Vanille, grilled fish, chicken curry, or tomato relishes
recipe.story: Pilao ya Nazi lets coconut do the quiet work. The rice steams with warm spices until the grains are fragrant, creamy at the edges, and ready to carry seafood or stew.
recipe.regional_variations: Some versions add meat or fish, while this one stays as a coconut rice side.

replace_steps.title: Bloom the spices
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil and cook onion until golden. Add cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and turmeric, then stir until fragrant.

replace_steps.title: Toast the rice
replace_steps.instruction: Add rinsed rice and stir so every grain is coated in the spiced oil.

replace_steps.title: Steam
replace_steps.instruction: Add coconut milk, water, and salt. Bring to a boil, cover tightly, and cook on the lowest heat until the liquid is absorbed.

replace_steps.title: Rest
replace_steps.instruction: Take the pot off the heat and rest covered so the grains finish steaming.

replace_steps.title: Fluff and serve
replace_steps.instruction: Fluff gently, remove whole spices if desired, and serve with seafood, chicken, or vegetable stew.

liboke-ya-mbika-cd.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Congolese references support liboke as banana-leaf parcel cooking and mbika as pumpkin seed pudding.
liboke-ya-mbika-cd.review_summary: Liboke ya Mbika is directionally sound as pumpkin seed paste steamed in banana leaves. It needed salt and a small blending-liquid cue made visible.
recommended_changes: Add salt and a little water for grinding.
Keep banana leaf steaming as the core technique.

recipe.story: Liboke is the art of letting banana leaves season the food while they protect it. Here, ground pumpkin seeds steam into a rich mbika parcel with tomato, onion, palm oil, and chile.
recipe.regional_variations: Fish, pork, cassava leaves, and pumpkin seed parcels all sit inside the wider liboke family.

moambe-chicken-cd.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Multiple Congolese and Central African references identify chicken in palm nut sauce as a national or iconic dish.
moambe-chicken-cd.review_summary: Moambe Chicken is sound as chicken in palm nut sauce, but the page omitted oil, salt, pepper, and an optional okra cue found in Congolese versions.
recommended_changes: Add oil, salt, pepper, and optional okra.
Keep palm nut cream as the heart of the sauce.

recipe.description: Tender chicken simmered in palm nut sauce with tomato, onion, garlic, chile, and optional okra.
recipe.story: Moambe Chicken belongs to the Congo Basin table. The sauce should be deep orange, rich from palm nut cream, and thick enough to cling to chicken before it meets fufu, rice, or plantain.

replace_steps.title: Brown the chicken
replace_steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt and black pepper. Heat oil and brown the pieces on all sides, then remove them from the pot.

replace_steps.title: Cook the aromatics
replace_steps.instruction: Cook onions in the same pot until soft. Add garlic and tomato paste and stir until fragrant.

replace_steps.title: Build the sauce
replace_steps.instruction: Add tomatoes, palm nut cream, water, and okra if using. Stir until smooth.

replace_steps.title: Simmer
replace_steps.instruction: Return chicken to the pot, add whole scotch bonnets, cover, and simmer until the chicken is tender and the sauce is thick.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Remove the whole peppers, adjust seasoning, and serve with fufu, rice, or boiled plantains.

saka-saka-cd.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Congolese sources consistently describe cassava leaves, palm oil, onion, fish or meat, and often peanut paste.
saka-saka-cd.review_summary: Saka-Saka is right on cassava leaves, palm oil, fish, and long cooking. The page needed the common peanut paste option and more visible salt/water balance.
recommended_changes: Add peanut butter or ground peanuts.
Keep long cooking for cassava leaves.

recipe.story: Saka-Saka is a patient pot. Cassava leaves need time to soften, palm oil gives them depth, dried fish brings smoke, and peanut paste makes the greens round and satisfying.

replace_steps.title: Prepare the leaves
replace_steps.instruction: Pound or blend fresh cassava leaves very finely. If using frozen leaves, thaw and squeeze lightly.

replace_steps.title: Boil the greens
replace_steps.instruction: Simmer cassava leaves with water until they begin to soften.
replace_steps.tip: Cassava leaves are not a quick green. Give them time.

replace_steps.title: Build the flavor
replace_steps.instruction: In another pan, heat palm oil and cook onion until golden. Add garlic and fish, then stir into the cassava leaves.

replace_steps.title: Add peanut and slow-cook
replace_steps.instruction: Loosen peanut butter with hot cooking liquid and stir it into the pot. Add the whole chile if using and simmer until the leaves are tender and rich.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Remove the whole chile, season with salt, and serve with fufu, boiled cassava, plantain, or rice.

sources.title: Congolese cultural profile - Metro South Health PDF

djiboutian-laxoox.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Horn of Africa references support laxoox/lahoh as sorghum-based fermented flatbread cooked on one side.
djiboutian-laxoox.review_summary: Laxoox is sound as a fermented one-sided flatbread. The fermentation timer was missing and the page needed serving context.
recommended_changes: Add the fermentation timer.
Keep one-sided cooking and bubble texture.

recipe.best_served_with: Honey and ghee, spiced tea, fah-fah, or tomato stews
recipe.story: Laxoox is breakfast bread with bubbles. The batter ferments until lively, then cooks on one side so the top stays spongy enough to catch honey, ghee, or stew.

replace_steps.title: Mix the batter
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk flours, yeast, sugar, salt, and warm water until smooth.

replace_steps.title: Ferment
replace_steps.instruction: Cover and let the batter stand until bubbly and lightly tangy.
replace_steps.tip: A warm kitchen gives better bubbles.

replace_steps.title: Cook one side
replace_steps.instruction: Pour a thin layer into a warm non-stick pan. Cook without flipping until the top is full of small holes and the edges lift.

replace_steps.title: Stack
replace_steps.instruction: Lift the laxoox carefully and stack on a plate. Repeat with the remaining batter.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Serve with honey and ghee for breakfast, or use it to scoop soup or stew.

fah-fah.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Djiboutian food references support goat or meat soup with vegetables as fah-fah.
fah-fah.review_summary: Fah-Fah is sound as a Djiboutian goat or meat soup with vegetables and warming spices. It needed serving context and cleaner step titles.
recommended_changes: Keep goat or lamb bones for broth depth.
Add laxoox or bread as the natural serving partner.

recipe.best_served_with: Laxoox, crusty bread, rice, or lime wedges
recipe.story: Fah-Fah is broth with backbone. Bone-in goat gives the soup strength, potatoes and carrots make it a meal, and lime at the table wakes the whole bowl up.

replace_steps.title: Start the broth
replace_steps.instruction: Place goat pieces in a large pot with water. Bring to a boil and skim the foam.

replace_steps.title: Simmer the meat
replace_steps.instruction: Add onion, garlic, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and the whole chile. Simmer until the meat is tender.

replace_steps.title: Add vegetables
replace_steps.instruction: Add carrots and potatoes and keep simmering until the vegetables are tender.

replace_steps.title: Season
replace_steps.instruction: Remove the whole chile, season with salt, and check that the broth tastes full.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Ladle into bowls with meat and vegetables. Finish with cilantro and lime.

skoudehkaris.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Djibouti-focused culinary references consistently show lamb or meat, rice, tomato, cumin, cardamom, cinnamon, and chile.
skoudehkaris.review_summary: Skoudehkaris was missing tomato paste, chile warmth, black pepper, and herbs that appear in stronger Djibouti references. The one-pot lamb and rice base is sound.
recommended_changes: Increase tomato presence.
Add tomato paste, chile, black pepper, and cilantro.

recipe.best_served_with: Banana, salad, yogurt, or chile sauce
recipe.story: Skoudehkaris should smell like the port cities of the Horn: lamb, rice, tomato, cardamom, cumin, cinnamon, and a little chile all settling into one pot.

replace_steps.title: Brown the lamb
replace_steps.instruction: Season lamb with salt and black pepper. Brown it in hot oil until the edges take color.

replace_steps.title: Cook the tomato base
replace_steps.instruction: Add onions and cook until soft. Stir in garlic, tomatoes, tomato paste, cardamom, cumin, cinnamon, turmeric, and chile.

replace_steps.title: Simmer the meat
replace_steps.instruction: Add water or stock, cover, and simmer until the lamb is almost tender.

replace_steps.title: Steam the rice
replace_steps.instruction: Add drained rice to the pot. Cover tightly and cook on low until the rice absorbs the broth.

replace_steps.title: Rest and finish
replace_steps.instruction: Rest covered, fluff gently, scatter cilantro over the top, and serve with banana or salad.

ful-medames-eg.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Egypt-focused and museum references agree on fava beans, olive oil, cumin, lemon, and garlic.
ful-medames-eg.review_summary: Ful Medames is sound, but the cook time was inconsistent with the dried-bean method and salt was missing from the visible ingredient list.
recommended_changes: Set cook time to match dried fava beans.
Add salt and keep olive oil, lemon, garlic, and cumin central.

recipe.story: Ful Medames is everyday Egyptian comfort. The beans should be creamy but not erased, bright with lemon, warm with cumin, and glossy with olive oil.

sources.title: University of Michigan Kelsey Museum - Ful Medames PDF

koshari-eg.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Egyptian references consistently describe rice, lentils, pasta, chickpeas, tomato sauce, garlic vinegar, and crispy onions.
koshari-eg.review_summary: Koshari was missing several ingredients used in the method: oil, cumin, salt, chile, and enough vinegar for both tomato sauce and dakka. Vermicelli is also a common rice component.
recommended_changes: Add oil, cumin, chile, salt, and enough vinegar.
Add vermicelli for the rice layer.

recipe.story: Koshari is Cairo in layers: rice, lentils, pasta, chickpeas, tomato sauce, garlicky vinegar, and a crown of onions fried until crisp.
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks fold vermicelli into the rice, some keep the rice plain. The tomato sauce, dakka, and fried onions carry the soul either way.

replace_steps.title: Cook the lentils
replace_steps.instruction: Boil lentils in salted water until tender but still holding shape. Drain and set aside.

replace_steps.title: Cook rice and pasta
replace_steps.instruction: Toast vermicelli in a little onion oil if using, then cook with rice. Boil macaroni separately until al dente.

replace_steps.title: Fry the onions
replace_steps.instruction: Fry sliced onions in batches until deep golden and crisp. Drain well and keep the onion oil.

replace_steps.title: Make tomato sauce
replace_steps.instruction: Cook half the garlic in a little onion oil. Add tomatoes, some vinegar, cumin, salt, and chile, then simmer until thick.

replace_steps.title: Make dakka
replace_steps.instruction: Sizzle the remaining garlic in oil, then add vinegar and cumin. It should smell sharp and garlicky.

replace_steps.title: Assemble
replace_steps.instruction: Layer rice, lentils, macaroni, and chickpeas. Spoon over tomato sauce and dakka, then finish with crispy onions.

sources.title: World Food Forum Recipe Booklet - Koshari

molokhia-eg.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Egyptian references agree on finely chopped jute leaves, chicken broth, garlic, coriander, and ghee or oil.
molokhia-eg.review_summary: Molokhia is sound, but the broth aromatics used in the method were not visible in the ingredient list. The public copy also needed cleaner punctuation.
recommended_changes: Add onion, bay, cardamom, salt, and lemon wedges.
Keep the garlic-coriander tash-a as the defining finish.

recipe.story: Molokhia is all about texture and aroma. The jute leaves make the soup silky, then hot ghee, garlic, and coriander hit the pot and announce dinner.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, vermicelli rice, roasted chicken, lemon, and bread

replace_steps.title: Make the broth
replace_steps.instruction: Poach chicken with onion, bay leaves, cardamom, salt, and pepper until cooked. Remove the chicken and strain the broth.

replace_steps.title: Cook the molokhia
replace_steps.instruction: Bring broth to a simmer, add chopped molokhia, and cook gently until the soup is silky. Do not boil hard.

replace_steps.title: Make the tash-a
replace_steps.instruction: Heat ghee, add garlic until golden, then stir in coriander for a few seconds until fragrant.
replace_steps.tip: Garlic moves from golden to burnt quickly, so stay close.

replace_steps.title: Finish the pot
replace_steps.instruction: Pour the sizzling garlic-coriander mixture into the molokhia and stir.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Serve with rice, chicken, lemon wedges, and bread.

jollof-rice-gq.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Equatorial Guinea food references support rice, plantain, tomato, fish/meat, and Spanish/Central African influence; broader jollof references support the tomato-pepper rice method.
jollof-rice-gq.review_summary: The page works as an Equatoguinean-style tomato rice, but the old ingredient list missed garlic, peppers, cooking liquid, salt, and pepper while the method already used some of them.
recommended_changes: Keep the tomato rice frame.
Make stock or water, seasoning, garlic, and pepper visible.
Treat smoked paprika as an optional Spanish-influenced accent rather than the heart of the dish.

recipe.description: A smoky Equatoguinean-style tomato rice cooked with peppers, palm oil, bay leaf, and a gentle Spanish paprika accent.
recipe.story: This is tomato rice with one foot in the Gulf of Guinea and one foot in Equatorial Guinea's Spanish-speaking kitchen. Cook the sauce down until it tastes rounded before the rice goes in.
recipe.best_served_with: Fried plantains, grilled fish, roast chicken, or a crisp tomato salad
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks use palm oil for a deeper color, while others use vegetable oil and keep the tomato-pepper base lighter.

replace_ingredients.name: long-grain rice
replace_ingredients.prep_note: rinsed until the water runs clear

replace_steps.title: Cook the base
replace_steps.instruction: Heat the oil in a heavy pot. Fry the sliced onion until soft, then add tomato paste and cook until it darkens slightly.

replace_steps.title: Reduce the sauce
replace_steps.instruction: Add the blended tomatoes, peppers, onion, garlic, bay leaves, paprika if using, and scotch bonnet. Simmer until the sauce thickens and the oil begins to show.
replace_steps.tip: The sauce should taste cooked before rice enters the pot.

replace_steps.title: Add rice
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in the rinsed rice until every grain is coated. Add hot water or stock and season with salt and black pepper.

replace_steps.title: Steam gently
replace_steps.instruction: Bring to a boil, then cover tightly and cook on low heat until the rice absorbs the liquid and becomes tender.
replace_steps.tip: Keep the lid closed so the rice steams instead of drying out.

replace_steps.title: Rest and serve
replace_steps.instruction: Turn off the heat and rest the pot for 10 minutes. Remove bay leaves, fluff gently, and serve hot.

sources.title: Travel Food Atlas - Equatorial Guinea Food
Chef's Pencil - Popular Foods in Equatorial Guinea

pepesoup-gq.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Country food references consistently describe pepesup as a spicy fish soup, often with plantain.
pepesoup-gq.review_summary: Pepesup is well supported as an Equatorial Guinean spicy fish soup. The old page was missing plantain, tomato, salt, and citrus even though those fit the strongest references and its own method.
recommended_changes: Add green plantain for the Equatorial Guinean pepesup pattern.
Add tomato, salt, and lemon or lime.
Keep the broth light, hot, and fish-forward.

recipe.description: A hot Equatorial Guinean fish pepesup with green plantain, tomato, ginger, chile, and a clear peppery broth.
recipe.story: Pepesup should wake the table up. Keep the broth clean, let the plantain soften, and lower the fish in gently so the pieces stay whole.
recipe.best_served_with: Steamed rice, boiled cassava, or extra green plantain

replace_steps.title: Season the fish
replace_steps.instruction: Season the fish with salt, a spoon of citrus juice if using, and a pinch of pepper. Rest while the broth starts.

replace_steps.title: Start the broth
replace_steps.instruction: Bring water, onion, tomato, garlic, ginger, ground spices, and scotch bonnet to a boil.

replace_steps.title: Cook the plantain
replace_steps.instruction: Add green plantain chunks and simmer until just tender.

replace_steps.title: Add the fish
replace_steps.instruction: Lower the fish pieces into the broth, reduce to a steady simmer, and cook until opaque.
replace_steps.tip: Swirl the pot instead of stirring hard so the fish stays intact.

replace_steps.title: Finish hot
replace_steps.instruction: Taste for salt and citrus. Break a pepper only if you want stronger heat, then serve the soup steaming hot.

sources.title: Global Diversity Hub - Famous Food in Equatorial Guinea

succotash-gq.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Food guides list succotash for Equatorial Guinea, but history-focused discussion treats the origin claim as disputed.
succotash-gq.review_summary: Succotash is repeatedly listed in online Equatorial Guinea food guides, but its history is disputed and not strongly documented as a precolonial local dish. The page should keep the bean-corn stew useful while avoiding overclaiming.
recommended_changes: Keep black-eyed peas and corn.
Add okra, seasoning, and herbs for a fuller vegetable stew.
Rewrite the story to acknowledge the disputed transatlantic frame.

recipe.description: A colorful bean, corn, okra, and tomato stew cooked with palm oil and chile in an Equatoguinean home-style frame.
recipe.story: Succotash has a complicated route through the Atlantic world, so cook this page as a practical bean-and-corn stew rather than a fixed ancient formula. The strength is in the contrast: creamy peas, sweet corn, tender okra, and tomato.
recipe.best_served_with: Fried plantains, rice, grilled fish, or roast chicken
recipe.regional_variations: Some versions stay simple with beans and corn, while others add okra, bell pepper, or green onion.

replace_steps.title: Cook the peas
replace_steps.instruction: Cover soaked black-eyed peas with fresh water and simmer until tender but not falling apart. Drain.

replace_steps.title: Build the base
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot. Cook onion until soft, then add garlic, tomato, bell pepper if using, and chile. Cook until the tomato turns saucy.

replace_steps.title: Simmer vegetables
replace_steps.instruction: Add cooked peas, corn, okra, and water or stock. Simmer until the okra is tender and the stew thickens.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Season with salt and black pepper. Scatter green onions over the top if using and serve hot.

sources.title: Travel Food Atlas - Equatorial Guinea Food
Fork and Salt - Equatorial Guinea Succotash

eritrean-ful.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Eritrean and broader ful references agree on fava beans, oil, cumin, chile, tomato, bread, and optional berbere.
eritrean-ful.review_summary: The ful page matches Eritrean and Horn breakfast patterns: fava beans mashed with cumin, olive oil, lemon, chile, tomato, berbere or awaze, and bread or injera.
recommended_changes: Keep the fava bean base.
Make the serving guidance stronger.
Add onion or scallion as an optional fresh topping.

recipe.description: Warm mashed fava beans finished with olive oil, lemon, cumin, chile, tomato, and berbere for an Eritrean breakfast bowl.
recipe.story: Ful is the kind of breakfast that can hold you until late afternoon. Keep some beans whole, mash some into the broth, then finish generously with oil, lemon, and fresh toppings.
recipe.best_served_with: Crusty bread rolls, injera, tomato, green chile, onion, tea, or coffee
recipe.regional_variations: Some ful houses add hard-boiled egg, yogurt, feta, awaze, or extra berbere at the table.

replace_steps.title: Warm the beans
replace_steps.instruction: Heat fava beans with a splash of their cooking liquid until hot. Mash about half the beans so the bowl turns creamy but still textured.

replace_steps.title: Season
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in garlic, cumin, berbere if using, and salt. Cook briefly so the garlic softens.

replace_steps.title: Finish with oil and lemon
replace_steps.instruction: Take off the heat and stir in olive oil and lemon juice. The ful should be loose enough to scoop with bread.

replace_steps.title: Top and serve
replace_steps.instruction: Top with tomato, chile, onion or scallion, and egg if using. Serve warm with bread or injera.

eritrean-shiro.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Eritrean/Ethiopian shiro references agree on shiro powder or chickpea flour, berbere, onion, garlic, water, and injera.
eritrean-shiro.review_summary: The shiro page is directionally sound, but the old default used niter kibbeh while also tagging the dish as vegan and dairy-free. Fasting shiro should use oil by default, with spiced butter as an optional non-fasting finish.
recommended_changes: Use oil as the default fat.
Keep niter kibbeh optional.
Keep the chickpea flour, berbere, onion, garlic, tomato, and water method.

recipe.description: Silky Eritrean chickpea-flour shiro simmered with onion, garlic, berbere, tomato, and oil until spoonable over injera.
recipe.story: Good shiro is smooth, not heavy. Whisk the powder in slowly, keep the heat gentle, and stop when it pours like thick cream over injera.
recipe.best_served_with: Injera, salad, sauteed greens, or a little extra berbere oil
recipe.regional_variations: Fasting versions use oil. Non-fasting versions may finish with tesmi or niter kibbeh.

replace_ingredients.name: shiro powder or roasted chickpea flour

replace_steps.title: Cook the onion
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot. Cook onion gently until soft and sweet, then add garlic and berbere.

replace_steps.title: Make the sauce
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in tomato paste and hot water. Bring to a simmer and season lightly with salt.

replace_steps.title: Whisk in shiro
replace_steps.instruction: Sprinkle in shiro powder slowly while whisking so it does not clump.
replace_steps.tip: If it thickens too fast, add hot water a splash at a time.

replace_steps.title: Simmer smooth
replace_steps.instruction: Cook on low heat, stirring often, until glossy and creamy.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Finish with niter kibbeh only if you are not keeping it fasting-style. Spoon over injera and serve hot.

sources.title: World Liver Day Recipe Book - Ethiopian Shiro Wat

zigini.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Multiple Eritrean zigni recipes agree on beef, onion, garlic, ginger, berbere, tomato, stock or water, and injera.
zigini.review_summary: Zigini is sound as an Eritrean berbere beef stew, but the old page used spiced butter while claiming dairy-free. Stronger references also commonly include tomato, stock, and browning the beef.
recommended_changes: Use oil as the default fat and leave spiced butter optional.
Add tomatoes or crushed tomatoes and enough stock for the braise.
Brown the beef before simmering.

recipe.description: A slow Eritrean beef stew with berbere, tomato, onions, garlic, ginger, and a thick red sauce for injera.
recipe.story: Zigini is not a quick red sauce. Brown the beef, let the onion and berbere bloom, then braise until the meat gives way and the sauce clings to injera.
recipe.best_served_with: Injera, salad, lentils, or sauteed greens
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks use tesmi or niter kibbeh, while many home versions use oil and keep the stew dairy-free.

replace_steps.title: Brown the beef
replace_steps.instruction: Season beef with salt and pepper. Brown it in hot oil, then remove from the pot.

replace_steps.title: Cook the onion base
replace_steps.instruction: Add onions to the same pot and cook until soft and beginning to brown. Stir in garlic, ginger, and berbere.

replace_steps.title: Build the sauce
replace_steps.instruction: Add tomato paste, tomatoes, and stock or water. Stir until the sauce is smooth.

replace_steps.title: Braise
replace_steps.instruction: Return beef to the pot, cover, and simmer gently until tender and the sauce is thick.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Taste for salt. Stir in tesmi or niter kibbeh only if using, then serve over injera.

incwancwa-sz.official_source_status: Limited official-style recipe coverage found. Eswatini food guides and cuisine references agree that incwancwa is sour porridge made from fermented cornmeal.
incwancwa-sz.review_summary: Incwancwa is supported as a Swazi sour fermented cornmeal porridge. The current recipe is usable; the main fix is clearer wording that it may be spoonable or thinned to drink.
recommended_changes: Keep fermented maize as the base.
Clarify the texture range.
Keep sugar optional.

recipe.description: A tangy Swazi fermented maize porridge, served softly spoonable or thinned into a refreshing sour drink.
recipe.story: Incwancwa is all about patience. Cook the maize porridge, let it cool, then give fermentation time to bring the clean sour edge.
recipe.regional_variations: Some households serve it thick like porridge; others thin it with water and drink it chilled or at room temperature.

sishwala-emasi-sz.official_source_status: Food guides and cuisine references agree on sishwala as maize porridge and emasi as sour fermented milk served with staple foods.
sishwala-emasi-sz.review_summary: The sishwala with emasi page is sound: stiff maize porridge served with sour milk is a core Swazi staple combination.
recommended_changes: Keep maize meal, water, salt, and emasi.
Strengthen serving copy and avoid overclaiming a single national dish standard.

recipe.description: Thick Swazi maize porridge served with cool tangy emasi, the simple pairing that makes the plate complete.
recipe.story: Sishwala should be firm enough to scoop, while emasi brings the cool sour balance. The dish is humble, filling, and deeply Swazi.
recipe.best_served_with: Umbhidvo, bean stew, grilled meat, or tomato relish
recipe.regional_variations: Some tables pour emasi over the porridge, while others serve it on the side for dipping.

sources.title: Eswatini Food Guide - Must See Spots

umbhidvo-wetintsanga-sz.official_source_status: Food guides and Eswatini cuisine references agree on pumpkin leaves or tops with groundnuts.
umbhidvo-wetintsanga-sz.review_summary: Umbhidvo wetintsanga is sound as pumpkin leaves cooked with groundnuts. The current page needed only a small liquid/texture cleanup and stronger serving guidance.
recommended_changes: Keep pumpkin leaves and groundnuts.
Add a splash of water or stock so the simmer step has liquid.
Keep onion and tomato as a common home-style version.

recipe.description: Tender Swazi pumpkin leaves simmered with tomato, onion, and crushed groundnuts until earthy, nutty, and soft.
recipe.story: Umbhidvo wetintsanga is garden cooking at its best. Young pumpkin leaves soften quickly, and crushed groundnuts turn the pot creamy without needing dairy.
recipe.best_served_with: Sishwala, emasi, beans, rice, or stewed meat
recipe.regional_variations: Some versions are only leaves and groundnuts; others add onion, tomato, or a little oil.

replace_steps.title: Prepare the leaves
replace_steps.instruction: Wash pumpkin leaves well, remove tough stems, and chop. Blanch briefly if the leaves are mature or hairy.
replace_steps.tip: Young tender leaves can go straight into the pot.

replace_steps.title: Crush the groundnuts
replace_steps.instruction: Pound groundnuts until coarse. Do not turn them into a smooth paste.

replace_steps.title: Cook the base
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil, soften the onion, then add tomatoes and cook until saucy.

replace_steps.title: Simmer greens
replace_steps.instruction: Add pumpkin leaves, crushed groundnuts, water or stock, and salt. Cover and simmer until the leaves are tender and the groundnuts turn creamy.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Serve warm with sishwala or another starch.

doro-wat.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Ethiopian and Eritrean references strongly agree on chicken, berbere, onions, spiced butter, eggs, and injera.
doro-wat.review_summary: Doro wat is sound: chicken, onions, berbere, niter kibbeh, tomato paste, lemon, and eggs. The old page incorrectly marked the default niter-kibbeh version as dairy-free.
recommended_changes: Remove dairy-free from default diet tags.
Keep dry-cooked onions, berbere, niter kibbeh, and scored eggs.
Add stronger serving guidance.

recipe.description: Ethiopian chicken and egg stew built on slow-cooked onions, berbere, tomato paste, and niter kibbeh.
recipe.story: Doro wat rewards patience. Let the onions reduce properly, bloom the berbere in spiced butter, then cook the chicken until the sauce clings dark red and deep.
recipe.best_served_with: Injera, ayib, gomen, lentils, or a simple tomato salad
recipe.regional_variations: For a dairy-free fasting-style adaptation, use spiced oil instead of niter kibbeh.

injera.official_source_status: Academic and culinary sources agree on teff fermentation, absit, sour batter, and one-sided cooking.
injera.review_summary: The injera page is sound but the old ingredient list made absit look like a second flour mixture. Stronger sources describe fermenting teff batter, cooking a portion into absit, then returning it before cooking one side only.
recommended_changes: Clarify absit as cooked batter or slurry returned to the fermented batter.
Include the long fermentation time in recipe timing.
Keep one-sided cooking and visible eye formation.

recipe.description: A tangy Ethiopian teff flatbread fermented for two to three days, finished with absit, and cooked on one side until full of eyes.
recipe.story: Injera is patient bread. Fermentation gives the tang, absit gives the soft elastic texture, and the covered pan gives the lacy eyes.
recipe.best_served_with: Doro wat, shiro, misir wat, tibs, kitfo, salads, and vegetable stews
recipe.regional_variations: Diaspora cooks often blend teff with wheat, barley, rice, or sorghum flour depending on climate and flour quality.

replace_ingredients.name: sourdough starter or reserved injera batter

replace_steps.title: Ferment the batter
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk teff flour with lukewarm water and starter if using. Cover loosely and ferment at room temperature until bubbly and pleasantly sour.
replace_steps.tip: Two days is mild; three days is tangier.

replace_steps.title: Make absit
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk about 1/2 cup fermented batter with 1 cup water in a small pot. Cook, stirring, until it turns glossy and thick. Cool until warm.

replace_steps.title: Finish the batter
replace_steps.instruction: Stir the cooled absit back into the fermented batter. Add salt and enough water for a thin crepe-like pour.

replace_steps.title: Cook one side
replace_steps.instruction: Heat a mitad or nonstick pan. Pour the batter in a spiral, cover, and cook until the surface is set and full of eyes. Do not flip.

replace_steps.title: Cool and stack
replace_steps.instruction: Lift the injera carefully onto a cloth. Cool fully before stacking so the breads do not stick.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Use one injera as the platter and roll extra pieces for scooping.

sources.title: Journal of Ethnic Foods - Injera Review

kitfo.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Ethiopian references agree on raw minced beef, mitmita, niter kibbeh, ayib, gomen, and Gurage origins.
kitfo.review_summary: The kitfo page matches the Ethiopian pattern: finely minced beef with mitmita and niter kibbeh, served raw, lightly warmed, or cooked with ayib and gomen. The main improvement is safer, clearer serving guidance.
recommended_changes: Keep beef, mitmita, niter kibbeh, ayib, and gomen.
Add explicit raw-beef handling guidance.
Name tere, leb leb, and cooked options clearly.

recipe.description: Finely minced Ethiopian beef seasoned with warm niter kibbeh and mitmita, served tere, leb leb, or fully cooked with ayib and gomen.
recipe.story: Kitfo is a confidence dish. The mince must be fine, the butter warm but not scorching, and the mitmita strong enough to perfume every bite.
recipe.best_served_with: Ayib, gomen, kocho, injera, or extra mitmita
recipe.regional_variations: Tere is raw, leb leb is briefly warmed, and yebesele is cooked through for guests who prefer it that way.

replace_steps.title: Prepare the beef
replace_steps.instruction: Use very fresh beef from a trusted butcher. Trim away sinew, keep it chilled, and mince finely with a sharp knife.
replace_steps.tip: If you are not comfortable serving raw beef, choose leb leb or fully cooked kitfo.

replace_steps.title: Make gomen
replace_steps.instruction: Saute onion and garlic in oil. Add chopped greens and cook until tender, then season with salt.

replace_steps.title: Prepare ayib
replace_steps.instruction: Season cottage cheese or ayib lightly with salt and lemon if needed. Keep it cool until serving.

replace_steps.title: Season kitfo
replace_steps.instruction: Warm niter kibbeh just until melted. Mix beef with niter kibbeh, mitmita, cardamom if using, and salt.

replace_steps.title: Choose the doneness
replace_steps.instruction: Serve raw for tere, warm briefly in a pan for leb leb, or cook through for yebesele.

replace_steps.title: Plate
replace_steps.instruction: Mound the kitfo with ayib and gomen beside it. Serve with injera or kocho.

poulet-moutarde-ga.official_source_status: No official Gabonese standard found. Gabonese cuisine references list mustard chicken with garlic, onions, and lemon juice; French poulet a la moutarde references support the mustard-chicken technique.
poulet-moutarde-ga.review_summary: The mustard chicken page is plausible for Gabonese/French-influenced cooking, but the old ingredient list missed salt and pepper while the method used them.
recommended_changes: Add salt and black pepper.
Keep mustard, lemon, garlic, onion, and chile.
Keep roasting as an easy home method.

recipe.description: Gabonese-style mustard chicken marinated with lemon, garlic, onion, chile, salt, and pepper, then roasted until golden.
recipe.story: Mustard does the heavy lifting here. Give the chicken time in the marinade, roast it hot, and spoon the pan juices back over the meat.
recipe.best_served_with: Rice, fried plantains, cassava, green salad, or tomato relish

replace_ingredients.name: chile flakes or minced fresh chile

replace_steps.title: Make the marinade
replace_steps.instruction: Mix mustard, lemon juice, garlic, oil, chile if using, salt, and black pepper.

replace_steps.title: Marinate chicken
replace_steps.instruction: Coat chicken well, cover, and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes.
replace_steps.tip: Two hours gives a deeper mustard flavor.

replace_steps.title: Roast
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oven to 200C. Arrange chicken skin-side up with onion rings and roast until golden and cooked through.

replace_steps.title: Rest and serve
replace_steps.instruction: Rest 5 minutes, then spoon pan juices over the chicken and serve.

recipe.story: Nyembwe is about the sauce. Brown the chicken, soften the aromatics, then let palm nut cream simmer gently until it coats every piece.
recipe.best_served_with: Boiled plantains, cassava, rice, fufu, or baton de manioc
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks season the chicken with mustard or bouillon; others keep the palm nut sauce simpler.

Frame it as a Gabonese everyday-style plate rather than a fixed national dish.

recipe.story: This is everyday coastal cooking: smoky fish, sweet plantain, and a quick tomato sauce that makes the plate feel complete.
recipe.best_served_with: Steamed rice, cassava, cucumber salad, or extra fried plantain
recipe.regional_variations: Some homes keep the sauce dry and chunky; others loosen it with fish stock and serve it over rice.

benachin-gm.official_source_status: No official standard found. Gambian food references agree on benachin as one-pot rice with fish or meat, tomato, vegetables, and rice.
benachin-gm.review_summary: Benachin is well supported as a Gambian one-pot rice dish related to thieboudienne. The old page had the right frame, but the ingredient list did not show oil, tomato, garlic, chile, salt, pepper, or cooking liquid clearly enough.
recommended_changes: Keep fish, rice, tomato paste, cassava, and sweet potato.
Add fresh tomato, garlic, oil, chile, salt, pepper, and cooking liquid.

recipe.description: Gambian one-pot tomato rice cooked with fish, cassava, sweet potato, cabbage, carrot, chile, and a rich seasoned broth.
recipe.story: Benachin means one pot, and the pot should taste complete before the rice goes in. Fry the fish, build a tomato broth, cook the vegetables, then let the rice absorb everything.
recipe.best_served_with: Lime wedges, fresh chile sauce, cucumber salad, or tapalapa bread
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks use fish, others use chicken or meat. Vegetables change with the market, but cassava, cabbage, carrot, and sweet potato are common companions.

domoda-gm.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Gambian references consistently describe domoda as groundnut stew served with rice.
domoda-gm.review_summary: Domoda is strongly supported as Gambian groundnut stew with beef or chicken, peanut paste, tomatoes, tomato paste, onion, pumpkin or sweet potato, chile, and rice. The old page needed salt, pepper, lemon, and water made visible.

recipe.regional_variations: Chicken, fish, beef, or vegetarian versions are all common. Pumpkin, sweet potato, okra, or bitter tomato can appear depending on the cook.

supakanja-gm.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Gambian cuisine references mention supakanja, and West African okra soup references support palm oil, okra, fish or seafood, and greens.
supakanja-gm.review_summary: Supakanja is plausible and locally recognized as Gambian okra soup. The old page had the core okra, palm oil, dried fish, shrimp, and pepper pattern but needed salt, water, and optional leafy greens made explicit.
recommended_changes: Keep okra, palm oil, dried fish, seafood, and chile.

Keep the draw texture as an expected feature, not a flaw.

recipe.description: Gambian okra soup with palm oil, dried fish, shrimp, chile, and a silky draw texture for rice or fufu.
recipe.story: Supakanja should be silky and generous. Slice the okra finely, let it draw in the palm oil, then add fish and seafood without overcooking them.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, fufu, cassava, or rice balls
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks add leafy greens, crab, smoked fish, beef, or extra dried seafood.

jollof-rice-gh.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Ghanaian and broader jollof references agree on basmati or long-grain rice, tomato stew, onion, oil, ginger, garlic, chile, and seasoning.
jollof-rice-gh.review_summary: The Ghanaian jollof page is sound. The main gaps were missing salt, stock or water, bay leaf, and optional curry or thyme while the method depends on seasoning and liquid control.
recommended_changes: Keep basmati rice and tomato stew as the Ghanaian frame.

recipe.description: Ghanaian jollof rice with basmati rice steamed in a reduced tomato, onion, ginger, garlic, chile, and bay-leaf stew.
recipe.story: Ghanaian jollof is all about the stew before the rice. Fry the tomato base until it tastes rounded, then let basmati steam gently under a tight lid.
recipe.best_served_with: Shito, fried plantain, grilled chicken, salad, or kelewele
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks use curry powder, thyme, nutmeg, or stock cubes. The rice is often perfumed basmati or another long-grain rice.

recipe.regional_variations: Chicken, goat, fish, smoked fish, garden eggs, or okra may move through the pot depending on the household.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Taste for salt and serve hot with fufu, omo tuo, or yam.

kelewele-gh.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Ghanaian food references agree on ripe plantain, ginger, chile, cloves or warm spices, salt, and frying.

fouti-gn.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Guinean cuisine references mention Foutti Fogne from Fouta, and fonio is strongly associated with Guinea and West Africa.

poulet-yassa-gn.official_source_status: No official Guinean standard found. West African yassa references agree on chicken, onions, lemon or lime, mustard, garlic, chile, and rice.

recipe.regional_variations: Senegalese, Gambian, and Guinean versions overlap, with ginger and chile levels shifting by cook.

recipe.regional_variations: Cassava leaves, sweet potato leaves, spinach, or mixed greens can be used depending on what is available.

caldo-de-mancarra-gw.official_source_status: Tourism and food references list caldo de mancarra as a characteristic Guinea-Bissau peanut broth with meat or fish.

Allow the groundnut broth to stay pourable for rice.

recipe.story: Caldo de mancarra should feel like a broth that became generous. Keep the peanut smooth, the chicken tender, and the rice ready to drink up the sauce.

recipe.regional_variations: Chicken, fish, shrimp, okra, carrot, or sweet potato may appear depending on the cook and coast.

replace_steps.instruction: Taste for salt and pepper. Serve hot over white rice.

arroz-de-caju-gw.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Guinea-Bissau references support cashews as a major crop and ingredient, but not a single fixed cashew rice formula.
arroz-de-caju-gw.review_summary: Cashews are central to Guinea-Bissau agriculture and food culture, but arroz de caju has thinner recipe-source coverage than the major national dishes. The page is useful as a cashew rice side, but it should avoid dairy-free conflict from default butter.

recipe.description: Long-grain rice folded with toasted cashews, onion, and light aromatics as a Guinea-Bissau-inspired side.

chabeu-gw.official_source_status: Tourism and encyclopedic references list caldo de chabeu as a characteristic Guinea-Bissau dish made with palm oil, okra, meat or fish.

recipe.name: Caldo de Chabeu

recipe.story: Chabeu is not rice pretending to be jollof. It is the sauce, red from palm oil and thickened with okra, made to ladle generously over rice.
recipe.best_served_with: Plain white rice, cassava, or boiled plantain
recipe.regional_variations: Fish, dried fish, chicken, beef, or shellfish can anchor the broth depending on the coast and household.
