replace_steps.instruction: Toss onion, tomato, cucumber if using, salt, lime, and a little piment. Keep some piment separate for the table.

replace_steps.title: Plate
replace_steps.instruction: Serve the attiéké with grilled fish, fresh garnish, and piment on the side.

sources.title: NKOSI - Recette Attiéké au Poisson Ivoirien

kenyan-pilau.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Kenyan cooking sources agree on rice, beef or meat stock, browned onions, garlic, ginger, and pilau spices.
kenyan-pilau.review_summary: The old pilau had the right ingredients but the sequence risked tough beef. The corrected method cooks the beef stock first, then builds the browned onion and spice base before steaming the rice.
recommended_changes: Simmer the beef first and reserve the stock.
Brown onions deeply before blooming pilau masala.
Serve with kachumbari to cut through the rich rice.

recipe.description: Kenyan beef pilau with browned onions, garlic, ginger, whole spices, pilau masala, and rice steamed in rich stock.
recipe.story: Good pilau starts before the rice. Build a proper beef stock, brown the onions patiently, and let the rice steam without too much stirring.
recipe.best_served_with: Kachumbari, lemon wedges, mango pickle, or plain cabbage salad
recipe.regional_variations: Coastal pilau leans aromatic and spice-forward; some Kenyan homes add potatoes, tomatoes, or extra chile.

replace_steps.title: Cook beef stock
replace_steps.instruction: Simmer beef with half the garlic, half the ginger, salt, pepper, and enough water to cover until nearly tender. Reserve the meat and stock.

replace_steps.title: Brown onions
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot and cook onions slowly until deep golden brown.

replace_steps.title: Bloom spices
replace_steps.instruction: Add remaining garlic and ginger, pilau masala, cumin, cardamom, cinnamon, and cloves. Stir until fragrant.

replace_steps.title: Coat rice
replace_steps.instruction: Return beef to the pot. Add drained rice and stir gently until each grain is coated in the spice oil.

replace_steps.title: Steam pilau
replace_steps.instruction: Pour in hot stock, adjust salt, bring to a boil, then cover tightly and cook on the lowest heat until the rice absorbs the liquid.

replace_steps.title: Rest and fluff
replace_steps.instruction: Rest covered off heat, then fluff from the edges inward. Serve with kachumbari.

sources.title: My African Food Map - Kenya Pilau

nyama-choma.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Kenyan and cultural sources consistently describe nyama choma as grilled goat or beef with ugali and kachumbari.
nyama-choma.review_summary: The old nyama choma was tasty but read more like a heavy marinade than the Kenyan classic. The corrected version keeps salt, smoke, optional lemon, and kachumbari as the core.
recommended_changes: Use goat or beef and keep seasoning restrained.
Make kachumbari part of the plate.
Remove halal as a diet tag because the recipe cannot guarantee sourcing.

recipe.description: Kenyan charcoal-grilled goat or beef, salted simply, roasted slowly, chopped hot, and served with ugali and kachumbari.
recipe.story: Nyama choma is about good meat, patient fire, and a fresh table. Keep the seasoning simple enough for the smoke to speak.
recipe.best_served_with: Ugali, kachumbari, sukuma wiki, and pili pili
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks add garlic, ginger, paprika, or lemon, but the classic Kenyan approach is salt, good meat, and charcoal.

replace_steps.title: Salt the meat
replace_steps.instruction: Rub meat with salt, pepper if using, and a little lemon or oil if you like. Let it sit while the fire settles.

replace_steps.title: Prepare charcoal
replace_steps.instruction: Build a charcoal fire and wait until the coals are glowing and ashed over. Set up a medium heat zone.

replace_steps.title: Grill slowly
replace_steps.instruction: Grill the meat slowly, turning often, until browned outside and cooked through near the bone.

replace_steps.title: Make kachumbari
replace_steps.instruction: Toss tomatoes, onion, chile if using, cilantro, lime juice, and salt. Keep it crisp and fresh.

replace_steps.title: Rest and chop
replace_steps.instruction: Rest the meat briefly, then chop into pieces and serve with kachumbari and ugali.

sources.title: Smithsonian Folklife Festival - Nyama Choma with Kachumbari and Ugali
The Domestic Man - Simple Kenyan Grilled Meat
Jikoni Secrets - How to Cook Nyama Choma

ugali-na-sukuma-wiki.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Kenyan food sources consistently describe ugali as stiff maize porridge and sukuma wiki as collard greens cooked with onion and tomato.
ugali-na-sukuma-wiki.review_summary: The existing ugali and sukuma wiki page was solid. This pass mostly tightens ratios, sides, and language so the greens stay bright and the ugali stays firm.
recommended_changes: Keep maize flour, water, and salt as the ugali base.
Keep sukuma wiki as collards or kale with onion, tomato, garlic, and oil.
Cook greens briefly enough to stay green.

recipe.description: Kenyan firm maize ugali served with sukuma wiki, collard greens quickly cooked with onion, tomato, garlic, and oil.
recipe.story: Ugali gives the plate its strength; sukuma wiki gives it life. Stir the ugali hard, then cook the greens quickly so they stay fresh.
recipe.best_served_with: Nyama choma, beef stew, beans, or kachumbari
recipe.regional_variations: Some homes keep sukuma wiki very plain with onion and salt; others add tomato, garlic, chile, or a little stock.

replace_steps.title: Boil water
replace_steps.instruction: Bring water and salt to a strong boil in a heavy pot.

replace_steps.title: Cook ugali
replace_steps.instruction: Add maize flour gradually while stirring hard. Keep folding and pressing until the ugali is firm and pulls from the sides.

replace_steps.title: Shape ugali
replace_steps.instruction: Turn onto a wet plate and shape into a dome with a wet spoon. Cover to keep warm.

replace_steps.title: Start greens
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil and cook onion until soft. Add garlic if using, then tomatoes, and cook until they break down.

replace_steps.title: Cook sukuma
replace_steps.instruction: Add sliced greens and chile if using. Toss until coated, then cover briefly until wilted but still bright.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Season the greens and serve hot beside the ugali.

sources.title: Learn About Kenya - Ugali with Sukuma Wiki

motoho-ls.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Food science and Basotho food sources describe motoho as fermented sorghum porridge or drink, with a sour slurry cooked into porridge.
motoho-ls.review_summary: The old motoho sequence cooked the porridge before fermentation, which is not the strongest fit for sour sorghum porridge. The corrected version ferments a sorghum slurry first, then cooks it into a smooth breakfast porridge.
recommended_changes: Ferment sorghum flour slurry overnight before cooking.
Use sorghum malt or a spoon of previous motoho as starter when available.
Make optional sugar a serving choice, not the base flavor.

recipe.description: Basotho sour sorghum porridge made by fermenting a sorghum slurry overnight, then cooking it smooth for breakfast.
recipe.story: Motoho needs time more than fuss. Let the sorghum sour gently overnight, then cook it slowly until the raw edge disappears.
recipe.best_served_with: Plain, lightly sweetened, or with milk offered separately
recipe.regional_variations: Some households back-slop with a little previous motoho; others use sorghum malt or natural fermentation.

replace_ingredients.name: sorghum malt or previous motoho starter

replace_steps.title: Make slurry
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk sorghum flour with lukewarm water until smooth. Stir in sorghum malt or starter if using.

replace_steps.title: Ferment
replace_steps.instruction: Cover loosely and leave in a warm place until pleasantly sour and lightly bubbly.

replace_steps.title: Cook porridge
replace_steps.instruction: Bring the remaining water to a boil. Whisk in the fermented slurry slowly and reduce the heat.

replace_steps.title: Simmer smooth
replace_steps.instruction: Simmer, stirring often, until the porridge is smooth and the raw sorghum taste is gone.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Season lightly with salt or sugar if using. Serve warm, or cool to drink as a thinner sour porridge.

sources.title: World Food Map - Fermented Sorghum Porridge

nyekoe-ls.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Lesotho food references consistently describe nyekoe as a beans, sorghum, and pumpkin or grain stew.
nyekoe-ls.review_summary: The old nyekoe leaned toward samp and beans, but stronger Basotho references point to beans, sorghum, and pumpkin as the more distinctive nyekoe profile. The corrected version uses that highland combination.
recommended_changes: Use sorghum grain, dried beans, and pumpkin.
Keep the dish thick and gently mashed.
Let soaking and slow simmering do the work.

recipe.description: Basotho bean, sorghum, and pumpkin stew simmered slowly until thick, earthy, and gently sweet.
recipe.story: Nyekoe is patient mountain food. Soak the beans, simmer the sorghum, and add pumpkin late so it softens without disappearing completely.
recipe.best_served_with: Papa, moroho, chile relish, or sour milk on the side
recipe.regional_variations: Some homes use samp or cracked maize in place of sorghum; pumpkin or potatoes are common additions for sweetness and body.

replace_steps.title: Soak beans
replace_steps.instruction: Cover beans with plenty of water and soak overnight. Drain before cooking.

replace_steps.title: Start grains
replace_steps.instruction: Combine beans, sorghum, onion, oil, and water in a heavy pot. Bring to a boil.

replace_steps.title: Simmer slowly
replace_steps.instruction: Reduce heat and simmer until the beans and sorghum are nearly tender, adding water as needed.

replace_steps.title: Add pumpkin
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in pumpkin and continue cooking until it is soft and the stew is thick.

replace_steps.title: Season and mash
replace_steps.instruction: Season with salt and pepper. Mash a small portion against the pot to make the stew creamy.

sources.title: World Food and Wine - Food in Lesotho
Sunheron - What to Eat in Lesotho

papa-le-moroho-ls.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Aid, food, and cultural references consistently describe papa with cooked greens as a staple Basotho meal.
papa-le-moroho-ls.review_summary: The existing papa le moroho recipe was close. The update keeps the everyday Basotho structure while improving water ratios, seasoning, and the greens method.
recommended_changes: Keep papa as stiff maize meal porridge.
Cook moroho as leafy greens with onion, tomato, oil, and salt.
Avoid overcomplicating a simple daily plate.

recipe.description: Lesotho's everyday plate of firm maize papa served with moroho, leafy greens cooked with onion, tomato, oil, and salt.
recipe.story: Papa le moroho is simple by design. Make the papa firm enough to scoop and keep the greens tender, seasoned, and generous.
recipe.best_served_with: Beans, stewed meat, eggs, or tomato relish
recipe.regional_variations: Moroho may be wild greens, spinach, chard, or cabbage depending on season and household.

replace_steps.title: Start papa
replace_steps.instruction: Bring most of the water to a boil. Mix a little maize meal with cool water to make a slurry, then stir it into the pot.

replace_steps.title: Thicken papa
replace_steps.instruction: Add the remaining maize meal gradually, stirring hard until very thick and pulling from the pot.

replace_steps.title: Steam papa
replace_steps.instruction: Cover and steam the papa on low heat so the meal cooks through.

replace_steps.title: Cook tomato base
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan. Cook onion until soft, then add tomatoes and salt and cook until saucy.

replace_steps.title: Cook moroho
replace_steps.instruction: Add chopped greens and cook until tender. Add a splash of water only if the pan dries out.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Serve a mound of papa with moroho alongside or spooned over the top.

sources.title: Help Lesotho - How to Make Papa
FAO Lesotho Home Gardening Poster - Papa le Moroho
World Food and Wine - Food in Lesotho

jollof-rice-lr.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Liberian jollof sources support meat or seafood, tomato paste, tomatoes, onions, peppers, stock, vegetables, and thyme.
jollof-rice-lr.review_summary: The old Liberian jollof was missing several common aromatics and vegetables. The corrected version keeps the Liberian mixed-protein feel while adding bell pepper, garlic, stock, thyme, bay, carrot, green beans, and cabbage.
recommended_changes: Add garlic, bell pepper, stock, thyme, bay leaf, and mixed vegetables.
Remove halal because smoked meats and sourcing vary.
Keep fried plantain and pepper sauce as serving notes.

recipe.description: Liberian jollof rice with chicken, smoked turkey, tomato stew, bell pepper, stock, thyme, bay, and mixed vegetables.
recipe.story: Liberian jollof is generous. The rice should taste of tomato stew and meat stock, with vegetables folded through the pot.
recipe.best_served_with: Fried plantain, pepper sauce, boiled egg, or cabbage salad
recipe.regional_variations: Proteins vary by home: chicken, smoked turkey, beef, shrimp, fish, or a meatless vegetable version.

replace_steps.title: Cook protein
replace_steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt and pepper, simmer until nearly tender, and reserve the stock. Add smoked turkey or fish later so it does not break apart.

replace_steps.title: Fry tomato base
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil and cook onions and bell pepper until soft. Add garlic and tomato paste and fry until the paste darkens.

replace_steps.title: Cook stew
replace_steps.instruction: Add tomatoes, thyme, bay leaves, chile if using, and some stock. Simmer until the sauce thickens.

replace_steps.title: Add rice
replace_steps.instruction: Stir rice into the sauce, then add chicken, smoked protein, vegetables, and enough stock to cook the rice.

replace_steps.title: Steam rice
replace_steps.instruction: Cover tightly and cook on low until the rice is tender and the liquid is absorbed.

replace_steps.title: Rest and serve
replace_steps.instruction: Rest covered, fluff gently, and serve with fried plantain and pepper sauce.

palm-butter-soup-lr.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Liberian sources consistently describe palm butter soup as palm fruit base simmered with meat, fish, seafood, chile, and rice or fufu.
palm-butter-soup-lr.review_summary: The old palm butter soup captured the main idea but needed stronger seasoning and liquid guidance. The corrected version makes palm fruit concentrate, meat, smoked fish, dried shrimp, chile, onion, and okra or bitterball cook as a coherent Liberian soup.
recommended_changes: Add garlic, dried shrimp or crayfish, stock or water, and salt.
Simmer until the palm oil rises and the soup thickens.
Keep rice or fufu as the main pairing.

recipe.description: Liberian palm butter soup with palm fruit concentrate, beef or goat, smoked fish, dried shrimp, chile, okra, and rice.
recipe.story: Palm butter soup should be deep orange, glossy, and rich. Give the palm base time to simmer until the oil rises and the smoke from the fish seasons the whole pot.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, fufu, dumboy, or boiled cassava
recipe.regional_variations: Fresh palm nuts make the deepest flavor, but palm fruit concentrate is the practical home shortcut.

replace_steps.title: Cook meat
replace_steps.instruction: Season meat with salt, pepper, onion, and garlic. Simmer with water until nearly tender, then reserve the stock.

replace_steps.title: Start palm base
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk palm fruit concentrate with stock in a large pot and bring to a simmer.

replace_steps.title: Add proteins
replace_steps.instruction: Add cooked meat, smoked fish, dried shrimp if using, onions, garlic, and peppers.

replace_steps.title: Simmer glossy
replace_steps.instruction: Simmer until the soup thickens and red oil rises to the surface. Stir occasionally so it does not catch.

replace_steps.title: Finish vegetables
replace_steps.instruction: Add okra or bitterball if using and cook until tender. Adjust salt and serve over rice.

sources.title: African Food Recipes - Liberian Palm Butter Soup
Recipe Buster - Liberian Palm Butter Soup
Peace Corps Cooking - Liberia Palm Butter Soup

torborgee-lr.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Liberian food sources describe torborgee as a thick, spicy, palm-oily stew associated with beans, bitter vegetables, dried proteins, and rice.
torborgee-lr.review_summary: The old torborgee page overfocused on locust beans and did not reflect the better documented Liberian beans or bitter vegetable torborgee pattern. The corrected version makes it a thick palm-oil stew of red cowpeas or lentils, bitter eggplant, dried seafood, hot pepper, and rice.
recommended_changes: Use red cowpeas or lentils as the base, with bitter eggplant when available.
Keep palm oil, dried shrimp or fish, onion, garlic, and chile.
Remove pescatarian because meat or mixed proteins are common.

recipe.description: Liberian torborgee, a fiery palm-oil stew of red cowpeas or lentils, bitter eggplant, dried seafood, onion, garlic, and chile.
recipe.story: Torborgee is not shy food. It should be thick, red with palm oil, bitter around the edges, and hot enough that plain rice becomes necessary.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, fufu, or boiled cassava
recipe.regional_variations: Some versions use red cowpeas, lentils, or African pea eggplants; proteins may include dried fish, shrimp, chicken, smoked meat, or no meat.

replace_ingredients.name: African pea eggplant or bitter eggplant

replace_steps.title: Cook beans
replace_steps.instruction: Simmer cowpeas or lentils in water until tender but not falling apart. Reserve some cooking liquid.

replace_steps.title: Build palm base
replace_steps.instruction: Heat palm oil and cook onions until soft. Add garlic and peppers and stir until fragrant.

replace_steps.title: Add bitter vegetable
replace_steps.instruction: Add bitter eggplant if using and cook until it begins to soften.

replace_steps.title: Add proteins and beans
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in dried fish, dried shrimp if using, cooked beans, and enough cooking liquid for a thick stew.

replace_steps.title: Simmer thick
replace_steps.instruction: Simmer until the palm oil colors the stew deeply and the texture is thick enough to spoon over rice.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Adjust salt and pepper. Serve in a small, intense portion over plenty of white rice.

sources.title: The Closing Argument - Popular Liberian Dishes

asida-ly.official_source_status: No single official recipe standard found. Libyan and regional references consistently describe asida as a flour-and-water mound served with date syrup or honey and butter.
asida-ly.review_summary: The existing asida was broadly correct. This pass adds date syrup as the more Libyan serving option and keeps the flour-water-butter structure simple.
recommended_changes: Use flour, water, and salt for the mound.
Serve with date syrup, honey, and melted butter.
Keep the method vigorous so the dough turns smooth.

recipe.description: Libyan asida, a smooth wheat-flour mound served warm with a well of melted butter, date syrup, or honey.
recipe.story: Asida is humble but not careless. Stir hard until the flour turns elastic, then shape it while warm and let the syrup pool in the center.
recipe.best_served_with: Mint tea, Arabic coffee, date syrup, or honey

bazin-ly.official_source_status: No single official recipe standard found. Libyan sources agree on barley flour bazin with lamb or mutton tomato sauce, potatoes, and eggs.
bazin-ly.review_summary: Bazin was mostly sound, but the spice profile was too thin for Libyan sauce. The update keeps barley dough, lamb tomato stew, potato, eggs, and adds the expected warm spices.
recommended_changes: Keep barley flour dough and lamb tomato sauce.
Add paprika, cayenne, black pepper, and fenugreek as Libyan sauce notes.
Remove halal as a guaranteed diet tag.

recipe.description: Libyan bazin, a dense barley-flour dome surrounded by lamb tomato sauce, potatoes, eggs, and warm spices.
recipe.story: Bazin is communal food. The dough should be firm enough to tear, and the sauce should be rich enough to season every bite.
recipe.best_served_with: Hard-boiled eggs, potatoes, lamb sauce, and simple salad

sources.title: AP News - Libyan bazin Ramadan dish

sharba-libiya-ly.official_source_status: No single official recipe standard found. Libyan references consistently describe sharba as lamb or beef soup with tomato paste, orzo, mint, spices, and herbs.
sharba-libiya-ly.review_summary: Sharba Libiya needed missing liquid, oil, chickpeas, lemon, and parsley/cilantro details. The update keeps lamb, tomato paste, orzo, dried mint, and Ramadan-soup identity.
recommended_changes: Add olive oil, water or stock, chickpeas, lemon, parsley, and salt.
Keep dried mint as essential finish.
Use orzo or small bird-tongue pasta.

recipe.description: Libyan lamb and orzo soup with tomato paste, chickpeas, cinnamon, turmeric, dried mint, lemon, and fresh herbs.
recipe.story: Sharba Libiya is the soup people mean when they simply say sharba. It should be meaty, tomato-red, minty, and bright with lemon.
recipe.best_served_with: Bread, dates, lemon wedges, and extra dried mint

replace_steps.title: Brown meat
replace_steps.instruction: Heat olive oil and brown the lamb or beef. Season lightly with salt and pepper.

replace_steps.title: Build base
replace_steps.instruction: Add onion, tomato paste, turmeric, cinnamon, and half the dried mint. Cook until the paste darkens.

replace_steps.title: Simmer soup
replace_steps.instruction: Add chickpeas and water or stock. Simmer until the meat is tender.

replace_steps.title: Add pasta
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in orzo and cook until tender, stirring often so it does not stick.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in lemon juice, parsley or cilantro, and remaining dried mint. Adjust salt and serve hot.

sources.title: Milk Street/AP - Libyan beef and chickpea soup

mofo-gasy.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Malagasy sources agree on sweet rice-flour batter cooked in round molds, with regional coconut milk variations.
mofo-gasy.review_summary: Mofo Gasy was ingredient-correct but the total time ignored fermentation. The update keeps rice flour, yeast, sugar, coconut milk, and mold cooking while making the rise time honest.
recommended_changes: Include one hour rise in prep time.
Keep rice flour and coconut milk as a common coastal/home variation.
Cook in round molds or an aebleskiver pan.

recipe.description: Malagasy sweet rice-flour breakfast cakes, fermented lightly, cooked in round molds, and served warm with coffee or tea.
recipe.story: Mofo gasy should be softly sweet and lightly yeasted. Give the batter time to wake up before it hits the hot mold.
recipe.best_served_with: Coffee, tea, honey, or fruit jam

ravitoto.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Malagasy recipes consistently use pounded cassava leaves with pork, garlic, and a long simmer, often with coconut milk.
ravitoto.review_summary: Ravitoto needed a longer braise time. Cassava leaves and pork should cook slowly until tender, rich, and earthy.
recommended_changes: Raise cook time for the long cassava-leaf braise.
Keep pork, cassava leaves, garlic, onion, ginger, coconut milk, and rice pairing.
Remove rushed 60-minute total behavior.

recipe.description: Malagasy pounded cassava leaves braised slowly with pork, garlic, ginger, and coconut milk until deep and earthy.
recipe.story: Ravitoto rewards patience. Cook the cassava leaves until their rough edge softens and the pork becomes part of the sauce.
recipe.best_served_with: Steamed rice and tomato rougail

replace_steps.title: Brown pork
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot and brown pork until the fat begins to render.

replace_steps.title: Cook aromatics
replace_steps.instruction: Add onion, garlic, and ginger. Cook until soft, then add tomatoes if using.

replace_steps.title: Add cassava leaves
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in pounded cassava leaves and water. Mix well with the pork and aromatics.

replace_steps.title: Braise slowly
replace_steps.instruction: Cover and simmer on low, stirring occasionally, until the leaves are very tender and the pork is soft.

replace_steps.title: Finish coconut
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in coconut milk and simmer uncovered until the sauce thickens.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Adjust salt and serve hot over white rice with tomato rougail.

romazava.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Malagasy references consistently describe romazava as beef or zebu with greens, especially anamalaho, served with rice.
romazava.review_summary: Romazava was close but needed anamalaho or paracress named clearly. The update keeps beef, tomato, onion, ginger, mixed greens, and rice, while avoiding extra vegetables that would drift away from the Malagasy profile.
recommended_changes: Name anamalaho or paracress as the distinctive green where available.
Keep rice as the natural pairing.
Remove halal as a guaranteed diet tag.

recipe.description: Madagascar's beef and greens stew with tomato, onion, ginger, and anamalaho or paracress when available.
recipe.story: Romazava is clean, green, and brothy. Let the beef become tender before the greens go in, then keep the final taste fresh.
recipe.best_served_with: Steamed white rice and tomato rougail

sources.title: US Department of State IVLP Cookbook - Royal Romazava

kondowole.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Malawian references consistently describe kondowole as a northern cassava-flour staple usually eaten with fish.
kondowole.review_summary: Kondowole was mostly correct. This pass keeps cassava flour and water, adds stronger fish-relish pairing language, and removes halal as unnecessary metadata.
recommended_changes: Keep cassava flour and water only for the staple.
Serve with fish, greens, or tomato-onion relish.
Keep the warning that it becomes very sticky and takes strength to stir.

recipe.description: Northern Malawian cassava-flour staple cooked into a sticky, elastic mound and served with fish or vegetable relish.
recipe.story: Kondowole is simple but physical. Add the cassava flour gradually and stir with confidence until it becomes smooth and stretchy.
recipe.best_served_with: Chambo, dried fish relish, mustard greens, or tomato-onion ndiwo

mandasi.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. East and Southern African mandazi/mandasi sources agree on flour, sugar, yeast or baking powder, milk or coconut milk, spice, and frying.
mandasi.review_summary: Mandasi needed honest rise time. The ingredients are plausible for a Malawian/East African fried dough, but the old 40-minute total ignored the one-hour proof.
recommended_changes: Include rise time in prep.
Keep flour, coconut milk or milk, sugar, yeast, cardamom, and frying oil.
Keep vegan only when coconut milk is the default.

recipe.description: Malawian mandasi, lightly sweet fried dough made with coconut milk, yeast, cardamom, and a proper rise before frying.
recipe.story: Mandasi should not be rushed. Let the dough puff, then fry gently so the inside cooks before the outside gets too dark.
recipe.best_served_with: Tea, coffee, fruit jam, or powdered sugar

nsima-chambo.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Malawi references consistently describe nsima as the staple and chambo as a prized Lake Malawi fish served grilled or fried.
nsima-chambo.review_summary: Nsima with Chambo was close, but it should name chambo as the preferred fish with tilapia as a substitute and should not advertise halal automatically.
recommended_changes: Use chambo where available, tilapia as substitute.
Keep nsima, lemon, garlic, tomato-onion ndiwo, and grilling method.
Remove halal metadata.

recipe.description: Malawian nsima served with grilled chambo or tilapia, tomato-onion ndiwo, lemon, and a firm maize-meal mound.
recipe.story: The plate is simple and proud: firm nsima, fresh lake fish, and a relish bright enough to pull everything together.
recipe.best_served_with: Tomato-onion ndiwo, mustard greens, lemon wedges, or Nali-style hot sauce

sources.title: Tellerrand Stories - Malawi's main dish Nsima

fakoye-ml.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Mali and Sahel references describe fakoye/fakou as a northern Malian leaf sauce from jute or similar leaves, often with meat or fish.
fakoye-ml.review_summary: Fakoye needed its leaf-sauce identity corrected. Okra can support texture, but the defining ingredient is fakoye or jute leaf, often with meat or dried fish and soumbala.
recommended_changes: Use fakoye, jute, or molokhia leaves as the base.
Keep soumbala/dawadawa and dried fish as common flavor builders.
Make okra optional instead of defining the dish.

recipe.description: Northern Malian fakoye leaf sauce with jute or molokhia leaves, dried fish, soumbala, onion, chile, and optional okra.
recipe.story: Fakoye is a leaf sauce first. The pot should turn silky and deep, with soumbala and dried fish giving it its old-school backbone.
recipe.best_served_with: Toh, rice, millet paste, or steamed fonio

replace_steps.title: Start base
replace_steps.instruction: Heat palm oil or shea butter and cook onion until soft. Stir in soumbala.

replace_steps.title: Add liquid and protein
replace_steps.instruction: Add water or stock, dried fish, and hot pepper. Simmer to season the broth.

replace_steps.title: Add leaves
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in fakoye leaves or powder. Cook until the sauce darkens and becomes silky.

replace_steps.title: Finish texture
replace_steps.instruction: Add okra if using and simmer until the sauce is lightly thickened. Adjust salt.

sources.title: Comboni Missionaries - Rice in Fakoye Sauce

tigadegena-ml.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Malian and West African sources agree on peanut paste, tomato, onion, meat or chicken, and vegetables.
tigadegena-ml.review_summary: Tigadegena needed garlic, liquid, and vegetable range. The corrected version keeps peanut paste, tomato, meat, and optional vegetables while making the sauce easier to cook without scorching.
recommended_changes: Add garlic, stock or water, and optional cabbage or sweet potato.
Loosen peanut paste before adding.
Remove halal as guaranteed metadata.

recipe.description: Malian peanut stew with lamb, beef, or chicken simmered in tomato, onion, garlic, groundnut paste, and vegetables.
recipe.story: Tigadegena is all about a smooth peanut sauce. Loosen the paste first, simmer low, and stir so the groundnuts do not catch.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, toh, fonio, couscous, or steamed millet

replace_ingredients.name: natural peanut paste or peanut butter

sources.title: The Food Dictator - Malian Peanut Stew

toh-ml.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Malian references consistently describe tô/toh as a millet, sorghum, or maize paste eaten with sauce.
toh-ml.review_summary: Toh was already in good shape. This pass keeps millet or sorghum flour, water, salt, and steady stirring as the full recipe.
recommended_changes: Keep millet or sorghum flour.
Use slurry first to reduce lumps.
Serve with tigadegena, fakoye, okra, or leafy sauce.

recipe.description: Malian toh, a firm millet or sorghum paste stirred smooth and shaped into a mound for sauces.
recipe.story: Toh looks simple, but the spoon work matters. Build it with a slurry, then stir until glossy and firm.
recipe.best_served_with: Tigadegena, fakoye, okra sauce, leaf sauce, or meat stew

lakh-mr.official_source_status: No single official recipe standard found. West African and Mauritanian references agree on sweet millet porridge or couscous served with fermented milk or yogurt.
lakh-mr.review_summary: Lakh was broadly sound, but this pass makes the millet-plus-fermented-milk identity clearer and adds common coconut, nutmeg, and baobab flour notes without forcing them.
recommended_changes: Use millet couscous or arraw as the base.
Pair it with sweetened fermented milk, yogurt, or curds.
Keep coconut, nutmeg, vanilla, and baobab powder optional.

recipe.description: Mauritanian-style lakh, soft millet couscous served with sweet fermented milk, yogurt, coconut, and warm spice.
recipe.story: Lakh should feel cool, creamy, and lightly tangy against the nutty millet. Let the grains cool before the milk goes on so the sauce stays fresh.
recipe.best_served_with: Mint tea, dates, roasted peanuts, or fresh fruit

replace_ingredients.name: fermented milk, lait caille, or plain yogurt

replace_steps.title: Steam millet
replace_steps.instruction: Steam the millet couscous over simmering water until the grains are tender and fluffy.

replace_steps.title: Cool and fluff
replace_steps.instruction: Spread the millet in a shallow bowl, sprinkle with salt, and fluff gently so it cools without clumping.

replace_steps.title: Sweeten milk
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk fermented milk or yogurt with sugar, coconut, vanilla, nutmeg, and baobab powder if using.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Spoon the cool millet into bowls and pour the sweet milk sauce over the top just before eating.

sources.title: Wikimedia Commons - Lakh fermented milk topping

thieboudienne-mr.official_source_status: Mauritania has regional versions of the wider thieboudienne family. Sources agree on fish, rice, tomato, vegetables, herb seasoning, and communal platter service.
thieboudienne-mr.review_summary: The Mauritanian fish rice was close, but it needed the herb stuffing, more vegetable range, and the one-pot tomato broth sequence that makes thieboudienne work.
recommended_changes: Stuff or rub the fish with parsley, garlic, chile, and lemon.
Use tomato paste, onion, cabbage, carrot, eggplant, okra, and root vegetables where available.
Cook the rice in the strained fish-and-vegetable broth.

recipe.description: Mauritanian fish rice with tomato-rich broth, fried fish, cabbage, carrots, eggplant, okra, and rice cooked in the sauce.
recipe.story: Good thieboudienne is built in layers: season the fish, cook the vegetables in the broth, then let the rice absorb everything left in the pot.
recipe.best_served_with: Chile paste, lime wedges, cucumber salad, or extra fried fish

replace_ingredients.name: firm ocean fish
replace_ingredients.prep_note: cleaned, scored, and cut in large pieces

replace_steps.title: Season fish
replace_steps.instruction: Pound parsley, garlic, chile, lemon juice, salt, and pepper. Rub it into the scored fish and rest briefly.

replace_steps.title: Brown fish
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil in a wide pot and brown the fish on both sides. Remove it before it flakes apart.

replace_steps.title: Build broth
replace_steps.instruction: Cook onions, tomato paste, and chopped tomatoes until the paste darkens. Add water or fish stock.

replace_steps.title: Cook vegetables
replace_steps.instruction: Add carrots, cabbage, eggplant, okra, and sweet potato or cassava if using. Simmer until just tender, then lift out with the fish.

replace_steps.title: Cook rice
replace_steps.instruction: Stir rinsed rice into the seasoned broth. Cover and cook low until the rice absorbs the sauce.

replace_steps.title: Assemble platter
replace_steps.instruction: Mound rice on a platter and arrange fish and vegetables over the top. Serve with chile paste or lemon.

mechoui-mr.official_source_status: No single Mauritanian recipe standard found. Country guides and regional references describe mechoui as an important whole-roast lamb or sheep dish.
mechoui-mr.review_summary: Mechoui was sound but too spice-heavy for the usual lamb-roast idea. This pass keeps cumin and butter while making the roasting/resting method clearer.
recommended_changes: Use lamb leg or shoulder when a whole lamb is not practical.
Keep seasoning simple with butter, cumin, garlic, paprika, salt, and pepper.
Roast slowly, then crisp and rest before tearing or carving.

recipe.description: Mauritanian-style mechoui, slow-roasted lamb rubbed with butter, cumin, garlic, and paprika until tender enough to pull apart.
recipe.story: Mechoui is celebration lamb. The magic is not a crowded spice drawer; it is patience, generous seasoning, and a final blast of heat for the edges.
recipe.best_served_with: Flatbread, couscous, salad, roasted onions, or mint tea

replace_steps.title: Make rub
replace_steps.instruction: Mix butter, cumin, paprika, garlic, coriander if using, salt, and pepper into a thick paste.

replace_steps.title: Season lamb
replace_steps.instruction: Score the lamb and rub the paste deeply into the cuts and over the surface. Rest at room temperature briefly or chill for a longer marinade.

replace_steps.title: Slow roast
replace_steps.instruction: Roast covered at 150C until the lamb is very tender, basting occasionally with pan juices.

replace_steps.title: Crisp outside
replace_steps.instruction: Uncover, raise the oven to 220C, and roast until the surface browns and crackles at the edges.

replace_steps.title: Rest and serve
replace_steps.instruction: Rest the lamb before carving or tearing. Serve with bread, couscous, salad, and lemon if using.

dholl-puri.official_source_status: No official standard found. Mauritian street-food sources strongly agree on yellow split peas, turmeric, thin griddle flatbread, and curry/chutney fillings.
dholl-puri.review_summary: Dholl puri was strong. This pass adds the reserved split-pea cooking water, finer yellow-pea crumb, and classic gros pois, rougaille, chutney, and achard serving set.
recommended_changes: Cook split peas until crushable but not wet.
Use the pea cooking water to soften the dough.
Serve thin, folded, and filled with bean curry, rougaille, chutney, and achard.

recipe.description: Mauritian dholl puri, thin yellow-split-pea flatbread folded around butter bean curry, rougaille, chutney, and achard.
recipe.story: The beauty is in the thinness. Grind the peas fine, keep the dough soft, and roll gently so the filling spreads without tearing.
recipe.best_served_with: Butter bean curry, rougaille, coriander chutney, achard, and chili paste

gateau-piment.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Mauritian recipes consistently describe yellow-split-pea chilli fritters fried until crisp.
gateau-piment.review_summary: Gateau piment was already close. This pass keeps the split-pea fritter structure and sharpens the street-food cues with curry leaves, spring onion, coriander, and baguette service.
recommended_changes: Use soaked yellow split peas ground coarsely.
Season with chile, onion, coriander, spring onion, cumin, and curry leaves where available.
Fry small and serve hot, plain or inside bread.

recipe.description: Mauritian gateau piment, crisp chilli fritters made from soaked yellow split peas, herbs, cumin, and green chile.
recipe.story: Do not puree the peas into baby food. A coarse paste gives gateau piment its crisp shell and tender, grainy middle.
recipe.best_served_with: Fresh baguette, butter, coriander chutney, or rougaille

replace_steps.title: Grind peas
replace_steps.instruction: Drain soaked split peas well and grind to a coarse paste, scraping down the bowl so some texture remains.

replace_steps.title: Season paste
replace_steps.instruction: Mix in onion, chilies, garlic, coriander, spring onions, cumin, curry leaves if using, baking soda, and salt.

replace_steps.title: Shape fritters
replace_steps.instruction: With damp hands, shape small balls or rough patties so they fry evenly.

replace_steps.title: Fry crisp
replace_steps.instruction: Fry in hot oil until deeply golden and crisp outside, turning as needed.

replace_steps.title: Serve hot
replace_steps.instruction: Drain well and serve immediately, plain or tucked into bread with chutney or rougaille.

sources.title: The Foodie's Avenue - Mauritian Gateau Piment
Royal Curry Club - Rougaille Gateaux Piment

rougaille.official_source_status: No official standard found. Mauritian references agree on tomato-based rougaille with onion, garlic, ginger, thyme, chile, and sausage or seafood variants.
rougaille.review_summary: Rougaille needed a more flexible Mauritian sausage note. This pass keeps the tomato-thyme-garlic-ginger sauce and allows pork, chicken, beef, or smoked sausages.
recommended_changes: Use Mauritian-style sausage, smoked sausage, pork, chicken, or beef sausage.
Cook tomato sauce down until thick, not watery.
Finish with herbs and serve with rice, dal, or dholl puri.

recipe.description: Mauritian sausage rougaille, a thick tomato, thyme, garlic, ginger, and chile sauce simmered with browned sausages.
recipe.story: Rougaille should taste bright and cooked down, not raw tomato. Let the tomatoes collapse before the sausage goes back in.
recipe.best_served_with: Steamed rice, lentils, dholl puri, cucumber salad, or achard

replace_ingredients.name: Mauritian, smoked, pork, chicken, or beef sausages

replace_steps.title: Brown sausage
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil and brown the sausage slices on both sides. Remove them so they do not overcook.

replace_steps.title: Cook aromatics
replace_steps.instruction: Cook onions in the same pot until soft, then add garlic, ginger, thyme, and curry leaves if using.

replace_steps.title: Reduce tomatoes
replace_steps.instruction: Add tomatoes and whole chile. Cook until the tomatoes collapse into a thick sauce.

replace_steps.title: Simmer sausage
replace_steps.instruction: Return sausage to the pot and simmer gently until the sauce coats every piece.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Remove the whole chile if you want a milder dish. Stir in coriander or parsley and adjust salt.

chicken-tagine-preserved-lemons-ma.official_source_status: No single official recipe standard found. Moroccan and cookbook references consistently describe chicken with preserved lemon, olives, onion, ginger, saffron or turmeric, herbs, and slow cooking.
chicken-tagine-preserved-lemons-ma.review_summary: The tagine was close. This pass adds parsley, olive oil, turmeric, and the bread-friendly serving cue, and removes couscous as the default pairing.
recommended_changes: Use chicken, grated or sliced onion, garlic, ginger, turmeric, saffron, preserved lemon, olives, parsley, and cilantro.
Cook gently until the onion sauce reduces.
Serve with Moroccan bread for scooping.

recipe.description: Moroccan chicken tagine with preserved lemons, olives, saffron, turmeric, onion, parsley, and cilantro.
recipe.story: The sauce should reduce into a glossy onion gravy. Add the preserved lemon and olives late so they perfume the dish without turning bitter.
recipe.best_served_with: Moroccan bread, cucumber salad, mint tea, or simple roasted vegetables

replace_steps.title: Marinate chicken
replace_steps.instruction: Rub chicken with garlic, ginger, turmeric, saffron water if using, salt, pepper, half the herbs, and olive oil.

replace_steps.title: Build tagine
replace_steps.instruction: Spread onions in a tagine or heavy pot. Add chicken and any marinade, then pour in a little water or stock.

replace_steps.title: Cook gently
replace_steps.instruction: Cover and cook over medium-low heat until the chicken is tender and the onion sauce has reduced.

replace_steps.title: Add lemon and olives
replace_steps.instruction: Add preserved lemon rind and olives. Simmer uncovered until the sauce is glossy and concentrated.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Scatter remaining parsley and cilantro over the tagine and serve with bread for scooping.

sources.title: Food Network - Chicken Tagine with Olives and Preserved Lemons
Cooks Without Borders - Anissa Helou Chicken Tagine
Cooking the Globe - Moroccan Chicken Tagine

harira-ma.official_source_status: No single official recipe standard found. Moroccan references consistently describe harira as a tomato, legume, herb, meat, and flour-thickened soup, especially during Ramadan.
harira-ma.review_summary: Harira was basically correct but needed celery, vermicelli, and clearer tadouira-style flour thickening. The update keeps chickpeas, lentils, tomatoes, herbs, spices, and lemon.
recommended_changes: Use chickpeas, lentils, tomato, onion, celery, herbs, ginger, turmeric, cinnamon, and pepper.
Add vermicelli near the end.
Thicken with a flour-water slurry and finish with lemon.

recipe.description: Moroccan harira, a Ramadan tomato soup with lamb, chickpeas, lentils, celery, herbs, vermicelli, and lemon.
recipe.story: Harira should be hearty but pourable. Let the legumes soften first, then thicken gently so the soup turns silky instead of pasty.
recipe.best_served_with: Dates, lemon wedges, chebakia, hard-boiled eggs, or crusty bread

replace_ingredients.name: plain flour
replace_ingredients.prep_note: mixed with water into a slurry

replace_steps.title: Brown base
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil and brown meat if using. Add onion and celery and cook until soft.

replace_steps.title: Spice tomatoes
replace_steps.instruction: Add ginger, turmeric, cinnamon, pepper, tomatoes, cilantro, and parsley. Cook until fragrant.

replace_steps.title: Simmer legumes
replace_steps.instruction: Add chickpeas, lentils, and water or stock. Simmer until the chickpeas and lentils are tender.

replace_steps.title: Add noodles
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in vermicelli and cook until tender.

replace_steps.title: Thicken and brighten
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk in the flour slurry gradually and simmer until silky. Finish with lemon juice and adjust salt.

msemen-ma.official_source_status: No official standard found. Moroccan references agree on layered square griddle flatbread made from flour, semolina, water, oil, butter, and salt.
msemen-ma.review_summary: Msemen was good. This pass emphasizes lamination with oil, butter, and semolina, keeps the square fold, and avoids overstating yeast as essential.
recommended_changes: Make a soft flour-semolina dough.
Rest well, stretch thin, oil generously, dust with semolina, and fold into squares.
Cook on a hot griddle and serve with honey, butter, or tea.

recipe.description: Moroccan msemen, flaky square griddle flatbread laminated with oil, butter, and fine semolina.
recipe.story: Msemen is all touch. The dough needs enough rest to stretch paper-thin, and enough oil and semolina to make the layers separate.
recipe.best_served_with: Honey, butter, jam, soft cheese, mint tea, or amlou

replace_steps.title: Knead dough
replace_steps.instruction: Mix flour, semolina, salt, sugar if using, and warm water. Knead until smooth, soft, and elastic.

replace_steps.title: Rest balls
replace_steps.instruction: Divide into small balls, coat with oil, cover, and rest so the dough relaxes.

replace_steps.title: Stretch and fold
replace_steps.instruction: Stretch one ball very thin on an oiled surface. Brush with butter-oil, dust with semolina, and fold into a square.

replace_steps.title: Cook griddle
replace_steps.instruction: Flatten each square gently and cook on a hot griddle until golden spots form and the layers puff.

replace_steps.title: Serve warm
replace_steps.instruction: Serve hot with honey, butter, jam, cheese, or mint tea.

caril-de-camarao.official_source_status: No single official standard found. Mozambican and Indo-Portuguese references agree on shrimp curry with coconut milk, tomato, aromatics, and curry spices.
caril-de-camarao.review_summary: The prawn curry was solid. This pass leans into the Mozambican coconut, tomato, curry spice, garlic, ginger, lime, and piri-piri profile.
recommended_changes: Keep prawns brief-cooked so they stay sweet.
Build sauce from onion, garlic, ginger, tomato, curry powder, turmeric, coconut milk, and piri-piri.
Finish with lime and coriander.

recipe.description: Mozambican caril de camarão, prawns simmered briefly in coconut milk, tomato, curry spice, garlic, ginger, and piri-piri.
recipe.story: The sauce can take its time, but the prawns cannot. Build the curry first, then add seafood at the end and stop as soon as it curls.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, xima, lemon wedges, or simple tomato salad

matapa.official_source_status: Mozambican references consistently describe matapa as cassava leaves cooked with ground peanuts and coconut milk, commonly seasoned with seafood such as shrimp or crab.
matapa.review_summary: Matapa had a dietary mismatch: it was marked vegan while still allowing shrimp. This pass makes the common seafood version explicit and removes the vegan tag.
recommended_changes: Use pounded cassava leaves, peanuts, coconut milk, garlic, onion, and piri-piri.
Include shrimp, dried shrimp, or crab as the common coastal version.
Cook cassava leaves long enough to soften fully.

recipe.description: Mozambican matapa, pounded cassava leaves simmered with ground peanuts, coconut milk, garlic, piri-piri, and shrimp.
recipe.story: Matapa should be creamy and earthy, with the cassava leaves cooked past toughness. The seafood seasons the pot rather than taking it over.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, xima, grilled prawns, or piri-piri chicken

replace_ingredients.name: shrimp, dried shrimp, or crab meat

replace_steps.title: Start leaves
replace_steps.instruction: Put pounded cassava leaves, garlic, chile if using, and water in a pot. Simmer until the leaves begin to soften.

replace_steps.title: Add peanut
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in ground peanuts and onion. Cook gently, stirring often so the peanut does not catch.

replace_steps.title: Simmer creamy
replace_steps.instruction: Add coconut milk and continue simmering until the leaves are tender and the sauce is creamy.

replace_steps.title: Cook seafood
replace_steps.instruction: Add shrimp, dried shrimp, or crab and simmer until cooked through and absorbed into the sauce.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Season with salt and lemon juice if using. Serve hot over rice or xima.

piri-piri-chicken.official_source_status: No single official standard found. Mozambican and Portuguese-influenced references agree on chicken marinated and basted with chile, garlic, citrus or vinegar, oil, and spice.
piri-piri-chicken.review_summary: Piri piri chicken was ingredient-correct but the page time ignored the important marinade. The update makes the African bird's-eye chile sauce and rest time explicit.
recommended_changes: Use a spatchcocked chicken for even grilling.
Blend piri-piri chiles with garlic, lemon or lime, vinegar, paprika, oil, and salt.
Marinate for at least four hours, then grill and baste.

recipe.description: Mozambican piri piri chicken, spatchcocked and grilled with African bird's-eye chile, garlic, lemon, vinegar, paprika, and oil.
recipe.story: The sauce is both marinade and basting brush. Let it sit with the chicken long enough to stain the meat, then grill until smoky and glossy.
recipe.best_served_with: Matapa, rice, fries, tomato salad, lime wedges, or extra piri-piri sauce

replace_steps.title: Blend sauce
replace_steps.instruction: Blend piri-piri chiles, garlic, lemon juice, roasted pepper, paprika, oregano, vinegar, olive oil, and salt into a punchy sauce.

replace_steps.title: Marinate chicken
replace_steps.instruction: Score the spatchcocked chicken, rub with most of the sauce, and chill for at least four hours or overnight. Reserve some sauce for basting.

replace_steps.title: Grill and baste
replace_steps.instruction: Grill over medium-hot charcoal, turning and basting regularly until the skin is burnished and smoky.

replace_steps.title: Check and rest
replace_steps.instruction: Cook until juices run clear and the thickest part reaches 75C. Rest before cutting so the juices settle.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Cut into pieces and squeeze lime over the top. Serve with rice, fries, matapa, or extra piri-piri sauce.

sources.title: The Guardian - Perfect Piri Piri Chicken

kapana-na.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Namibian references agree on grilled beef, kapana spice, and salsa made with tomato, onion, vinegar, oil, and chile.
kapana-na.review_summary: Kapana was close, but needed kapana spice in the ingredient list and the salsa should include oil as well as vinegar. Removed halal as a blanket promise.
recommended_changes: Season grilled beef with kapana spice.
Serve with tomato-onion-chile salsa dressed with vinegar and oil.
Keep the cook fast and hot like a market grill.

recipe.description: Namibian kapana, fast-grilled beef strips tossed with kapana spice and served with tomato, onion, chile, vinegar, and oil salsa.
recipe.story: Kapana lives on heat and speed. Grill the meat hard, slice it while hot, and let people dip into extra spice and salsa at the table.
recipe.best_served_with: Tomato salsa, extra kapana spice, vetkoek, pap, or oshifima

replace_ingredients.name: beef sirloin, rump, or game meat

replace_steps.title: Mix salsa
replace_steps.instruction: Combine tomatoes, onion, chilies, vinegar, oil, and a pinch of salt. Let it sit while the grill heats.

replace_steps.title: Season beef
replace_steps.instruction: Rub beef with oil, kapana spice, and salt. Keep extra spice nearby for dipping.

replace_steps.title: Grill hot
replace_steps.instruction: Grill over very hot coals until charred outside and juicy inside.

replace_steps.title: Slice and serve
replace_steps.instruction: Slice into bite-sized pieces and serve immediately with salsa and extra kapana spice.

oshifima-na.official_source_status: No official standard found. Namibian sources consistently describe oshifima as a stiff porridge made from mahangu, maize meal, or both.
oshifima-na.review_summary: Oshifima was already accurate. This pass keeps mahangu/pearl millet as the lead and clarifies that maize meal can be a practical substitute.
recommended_changes: Use mahangu or pearl millet flour as the main flour.
Stir hard in stages to prevent lumps.
Shape firm and serve with stew, greens, or grilled meat.

recipe.description: Namibian oshifima, a smooth stiff porridge made from mahangu or pearl millet flour and served as the base for stews and grilled meat.
recipe.story: Oshifima is simple but physical. Add flour in stages and stir with purpose until the porridge pulls from the pot and holds its shape.
recipe.best_served_with: Kapana, oshiwambo chicken, greens, beef stew, or tomato relish

replace_ingredients.name: mahangu or pearl millet flour
replace_ingredients.prep_note: maize meal can replace part of it

potjiekos-na.official_source_status: No official standard found. Southern African and Namibian references agree on a cast-iron potjie, layered meat and vegetables, slow outdoor cooking, and no stirring.
potjiekos-na.review_summary: Potjiekos was structurally right. This pass removes halal because wine is listed as optional, adds cabbage or pumpkin as expected potjie vegetables, and keeps the no-stir layered method.
recommended_changes: Brown meat before layering.
Layer slower vegetables lower and softer vegetables later or higher.
Do not stir during the slow cook.

recipe.description: Namibian potjiekos, a slow layered cast-iron stew of beef, onions, roots, vegetables, stock, and gentle fire.
recipe.story: A potjie is not stirred like stew. Build the layers, manage the coals, and let the pot steam and braise until the meat gives way.
recipe.best_served_with: Rice, oshifima, fresh bread, or simple salad

replace_ingredients.name: beef chuck, lamb, or game meat

sources.title: Tastes From The Road - Potjiekos From Namibia

dambou-ne.official_source_status: No official standard found. Niger references describe dambou as a Zarma/Songhai steamed cereal dish with moringa leaves, onion, chile, oil, peanut, and optional meat or fish.
dambou-ne.review_summary: Dambou was on the right track but too narrow. This pass adds onion, chile, crushed peanuts, and the key sequence of cooking greens separately before mixing with steamed grains.
recommended_changes: Use millet flour, rice flour, or couscous with moringa leaves.
Cook or blanch moringa separately before mixing.
Finish with onion, chile, oil, and crushed peanuts.

recipe.description: Nigerien dambou, steamed millet or couscous grains mixed with moringa leaves, onion, chile, oil, and crushed peanuts.
recipe.story: Dambou works best when the grains stay separate. Steam, break the clumps, then fold in the greens and seasoning while everything is warm.
recipe.best_served_with: Tomato sauce, grilled fish, roast meat, or extra chile oil

replace_ingredients.name: millet flour, rice flour, or fine couscous grains

replace_steps.title: Form grains
replace_steps.instruction: Sprinkle water into the millet flour or couscous while rubbing with your fingers to form tiny grains.

replace_steps.title: Steam first
replace_steps.instruction: Steam the grains in a lined steamer until firm and fluffy.

replace_steps.title: Cook greens
replace_steps.instruction: Blanch or steam moringa leaves until tender, then squeeze lightly if very wet.

replace_steps.title: Mix and finish
replace_steps.instruction: Break up the steamed grains and fold in moringa, onion, pepper, oil, crushed peanuts, and salt.

replace_steps.title: Warm through
replace_steps.instruction: Return to the steamer briefly so the flavors settle and the texture stays fluffy.

sources.title: Afrik Cuisine - Dambou Couscous with Moringa Leaves

kilishi-ne.official_source_status: No official standard found. Hausa and food-science references agree on thin dried meat coated with groundnut labu paste, spices, and further drying or roasting.
kilishi-ne.review_summary: Kilishi had the right idea but the total time was far too short for drying. This pass makes the Hausa labu paste, long drying, and final roast explicit.
recommended_changes: Slice very lean meat paper-thin.
Dry first, coat with groundnut labu paste, dry again, then roast briefly.
Increase total time to reflect real drying.

recipe.description: Nigerien-Hausa kilishi, paper-thin lean beef dried, coated in spicy groundnut labu paste, dried again, and roasted crisp.
recipe.story: Kilishi is preservation cooking. Thin slices, patient drying, and a well-seasoned labu paste make the meat crisp, spicy, and long-lasting.
recipe.best_served_with: Kunu, pap, garri, cucumber salad, or extra chile powder

replace_ingredients.name: very lean beef topside or flank
roasted groundnut paste or kuli-kuli powder

replace_steps.title: Slice meat
replace_steps.instruction: Trim all fat and slice the beef into broad, paper-thin sheets.

replace_steps.title: Dry first
replace_steps.instruction: Lay meat on racks and dry in strong sun or a very low oven until firm but still flexible.

replace_steps.title: Make labu
replace_steps.instruction: Mix groundnut paste with chili, ginger, garlic, onion, cloves if using, seasoning cube if using, salt, and enough water to spread.

replace_steps.title: Coat and dry
replace_steps.instruction: Brush labu paste over each sheet and return to racks until the coating dries and clings to the meat.

replace_steps.title: Roast crisp
replace_steps.instruction: Roast briefly over low coals or in a hot oven until aromatic and lightly crisp, then cool before storing.

sources.title: African Journal of Food Agriculture Nutrition and Development - Kilishi Processing

tuwo-masara-ne.official_source_status: No official standard found. Hausa-region references agree on maize flour cooked into a firm swallow served with soups and stews.
tuwo-masara-ne.review_summary: Tuwo masara was accurate. This pass keeps the slurry-first method and clarifies it as a northern Hausa-region maize swallow eaten across Niger and nearby countries.
recommended_changes: Use fine maize flour or cornmeal.
Start with a smooth slurry to prevent lumps.
Stir in more flour until the swallow is firm and glossy.

recipe.description: Tuwo masara, a smooth firm maize swallow from Niger and the wider Hausa region, shaped for dipping into soups and stews.
recipe.story: Tuwo rewards steady stirring. Start with a slurry, then add flour until the paste is thick enough to mound and pull cleanly from the pot.
recipe.best_served_with: Miyan kuka, okra soup, egusi soup, groundnut stew, or vegetable sauce

egusi-soup-ng.official_source_status: No official standard found. Nigerian sources agree on melon seeds, palm oil, meat or fish, crayfish or stockfish, peppers, and greens such as ugu, bitterleaf, or spinach.
egusi-soup-ng.review_summary: Egusi was mostly right but missing crayfish, stock, and a clearer pepper-and-palm-oil base. This pass keeps lumpy egusi and flexible greens.
recommended_changes: Use ground egusi, palm oil, pepper base, meat stock, stockfish, crayfish, and leafy greens.
Add egusi as paste spoonfuls for lumps or stir smoother for another style.
Serve with swallow rather than treating it as a stand-alone soup.

recipe.description: Nigerian egusi soup with ground melon seeds, palm oil, pepper base, assorted meats, stockfish, crayfish, and leafy greens.
recipe.story: Egusi can be smooth or lumpy. For this pot, spoon in a thick egusi paste and let the lumps set before stirring gently.
recipe.best_served_with: Pounded yam, eba, amala, semovita, fufu, or starch swallow

sources.title: All Nigerian Foods - Nigerian Egusi Soup
Kitchen Butterfly - Traditional Nigerian Edo Egusi Soup

jollof-rice-ng.official_source_status: No official standard found. Nigerian references consistently use parboiled rice, tomato and pepper base, onions, tomato paste, stock, thyme, bay, curry powder, and bouillon or seasoning.
jollof-rice-ng.review_summary: Nigerian jollof had good method but the ingredient list missed stock, thyme, bay leaves, seasoning cubes, and ginger or garlic used by many recipes. This pass fills those gaps and removes halal as a guarantee.
recommended_changes: Use long-grain parboiled rice.
Fry tomato paste and reduced pepper base well before adding rice.
Cook tightly covered with stock, thyme, bay leaves, curry powder, and seasoning.

recipe.description: Nigerian party jollof rice cooked in a fried tomato-pepper base with stock, thyme, curry powder, bay leaves, and a smoky bottom.
recipe.story: The sauce must fry until it tastes rounded before the rice enters. Tight cover, low heat, and a little bottom char bring the party flavor.
recipe.best_served_with: Fried plantain, chicken, beef, coleslaw, moin moin, or suya

replace_steps.title: Reduce pepper base
replace_steps.instruction: Blend tomatoes, red peppers, scotch bonnets, and one onion. Boil down until thick and not watery.

replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil, fry sliced onion, then add tomato paste and cook until darkened. Stir in the reduced pepper base.

replace_steps.title: Season sauce
replace_steps.instruction: Add curry powder, thyme, bay leaves, ginger-garlic if using, seasoning cubes if using, salt, and pepper. Fry until oil rises.

replace_steps.title: Add rice
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in washed rice until every grain is coated. Add enough stock to sit just below or level with the rice.

replace_steps.title: Steam rice
replace_steps.instruction: Cover tightly with foil and a lid. Cook low until the rice is tender, then fluff from the bottom.

replace_steps.title: Smoke bottom
replace_steps.instruction: Raise heat briefly at the end to toast the bottom lightly for party jollof flavor, then rest before serving.

sources.title: Tastes From The Road - Jollof Rice From Nigeria
University of West Georgia - Nigerian Jollof Rice Recipe

suya-ng.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Nigerian references agree on thin skewered meat seasoned with Hausa yaji, a peanut or kuli-kuli based spice blend, and grilled over fire.
suya-ng.review_summary: Suya was solid but needed kuli-kuli or peanut cake language and no halal guarantee. This pass keeps thin beef, yaji, charcoal grilling, onion, tomato, and cabbage.
recommended_changes: Use thin-sliced beef or ram on skewers.
Make yaji from kuli-kuli or roasted peanut powder, chili, ginger, onion, garlic, pepper, and salt.
Serve smoky with raw onion, tomato, cabbage, and extra yaji.

recipe.description: Nigerian suya, thin beef skewers coated in yaji, the smoky Hausa spice blend of kuli-kuli, chile, ginger, onion, garlic, and salt.
recipe.story: Suya should be sliced thin enough to cook fast, but not so thin that it dries out. The final shower of yaji is part of the dish.

recipe.name: Mbisi ya Makala
recipe.description: Congolese charcoal-grilled river fish with bright pili-pili sauce made from chile, onion, garlic, lemon, and oil.
recipe.story: Keep the fish simple and the fire hot. The pili-pili brings the bite, while plantain or chikwangue catches the juices.

replace_ingredients.name: tilapia, capitaine, or other firm river fish

poulet-muambe-cg.review_summary: Poulet muambe was sound. This pass removes the over-specific comparison line and reinforces palm butter, chicken, tomato, garlic, chile, okra, and stock.

recipe.best_served_with: Chikwangue, white rice, fried plantain, saka-saka, or greens

saka-saka-poisson-cg.official_source_status: Congolese references consistently describe saka-saka or pondu as pounded cassava leaves cooked with palm oil and often smoked fish, peanut, onion, garlic, and vegetables.
saka-saka-poisson-cg.review_summary: Saka-saka was correct, but the cassava-leaf cook time should be patient and the ingredient set benefits from leek or green pepper. This pass keeps smoked fish, palm oil, and peanut.

Add smoked fish late enough to stay distinct.
Use palm oil and peanut butter or crushed peanut for richness.

recipe.story: Saka-saka cannot be rushed. Cassava leaves need a long simmer before they become soft, savory, and ready to carry smoked fish.
recipe.best_served_with: Chikwangue, fufu, rice, plantain, or poulet muambe

igisafuria.official_source_status: No official standard found. Rwandan references describe Igisafuliya as a one-pot stew with chicken or meat, plantains, potatoes, vegetables, and tomato.

rwandan-brochettes.official_source_status: Rwandan recipe references consistently describe goat or beef brochettes grilled over charcoal and served with onion, tomato sauce, plantain, potatoes, or ugali.

chocolate-quente-st.official_source_status: No official São Toméan hot chocolate standard found. São Tomé is strongly documented as a cacao origin, and high-cacao hot chocolate references support cacao, water or milk, sugar, and spice.

yassa-poulet-sn.official_source_status: Senegalese sources consistently describe yassa as chicken or fish marinated in lemon, onion, mustard, garlic, and chile, then grilled or browned and simmered with onions.

Caramelize onions into a tangy sauce.

Simmer until the sauce is thick and glossy.

thieboudienne-sn.review_summary: Thieboudienne was strong but missing onion, oil, carrot, okra, tamarind, and dried fish in the ingredient list even though the method mentioned them. This pass fills out the Senegalese roster.

Use broken rice, tomato paste, vegetables, tamarind, dried fish, and fish stock.

Marinate with garlic, ginger, lemon or lime, and salt.
Serve with chunky tomato Creole sauce.

ladob.official_source_status: Seychelles references consistently describe ladob as either sweet or savory. The dessert version uses ripe plantains or sweet potatoes simmered in coconut milk, sugar, vanilla, and spice.
ladob.review_summary: Ladob was accurate for the dessert version. This pass removes halal as irrelevant, keeps coconut milk, vanilla, nutmeg, plantain, and sweet potato, and mentions the savory form only in context.

Simmer gently in coconut milk, sugar, vanilla, nutmeg, and salt.
Do not overcook into mush unless intentionally making a softer pudding.

recipe.description: Seychellois sweet ladob, ripe plantains and sweet potatoes simmered in coconut milk with vanilla, nutmeg, sugar, and salt.

shark-chutney.official_source_status: Seychelles references consistently describe shark chutney or satini reken as boiled, skinned, flaked shark cooked with bilimbi, lime, onion, chile, and turmeric.
shark-chutney.review_summary: Shark chutney was mostly right. This pass removes halal guarantee and adds a practical firm-fish substitution while keeping shark, bilimbi, lime, onion, turmeric, ginger, and chile.

akara-sl.official_source_status: Sierra Leone specific sources consistently describe banana akara as rice-flour and mashed-banana fritters, distinct from bean akara elsewhere in West Africa.

bariis-iskukaris.official_source_status: Somali references consistently describe bariis iskukaris as one-pot spiced rice with meat, xawaash, raisins, aromatics, and banana on the side.
