replace_steps.instruction: Whisk flours, sugar, yeast or starter, salt, and warm water into a thin smooth batter.

replace_steps.title: Ferment
replace_steps.instruction: Cover loosely and let the batter ferment until bubbly and lightly sour, several hours or overnight.

replace_steps.title: Adjust texture
replace_steps.instruction: Stir gently and add water if needed. The batter should pour more thinly than pancake batter.

replace_steps.title: Cook one side
replace_steps.instruction: Pour a thin layer into a lightly oiled pan. Cook without flipping until holes form and the surface sets.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Fold and serve warm with sweet toppings or with stew and suqaar.

suqaar.official_source_status: Somali recipe references consistently describe suqaar as small-cut meat sauteed with onion, garlic, peppers, tomato, and Somali spices, served with canjeero, rice, pasta, or bread.
suqaar.review_summary: Suqaar was close. This pass adds xawaash as the core spice cue, keeps peppers and tomatoes, and removes halal as a sourcing guarantee.
recommended_changes: Use small cubes of beef, goat, lamb, or chicken.
Season with xawaash, garlic, onion, peppers, tomato, and chile.
Cook quickly so the meat stays tender.

recipe.description: Somali suqaar, small-cut meat sauteed with onion, garlic, peppers, tomato, chile, and xawaash.
recipe.story: Suqaar is quick food, so cut the meat small and keep the pan hot. The vegetables should soften into a light sauce around the meat.
recipe.best_served_with: Canjeero, rice, pasta, sabaayad, banana, or basbaas

bobotie-za.official_source_status: South African references consistently describe bobotie as curried minced meat mixed with soaked bread and chutney, baked with an egg-milk topping and bay leaves.
bobotie-za.review_summary: Bobotie was strong. This pass adds vinegar or lemon for balance, keeps bread-in-milk and egg custard, and names yellow rice and sambals as the proper table.
recommended_changes: Use curried mince with soaked bread, chutney, raisins or sultanas, and bay leaves.
Bake under egg and milk custard.
Serve with yellow rice and sambals.

recipe.description: South African bobotie, curried mince with soaked bread, chutney, sultanas, bay leaves, and a golden egg custard topping.
recipe.story: Bobotie is all balance: curry warmth, fruit sweetness, a little acidity, and a custard top that sets softly over the mince.
recipe.best_served_with: Yellow rice, sambals, chutney, banana slices, or salad

bunny-chow-za.official_source_status: South African references consistently describe bunny chow as a Durban curry served inside a hollowed quarter or half loaf of bread.
bunny-chow-za.review_summary: Bunny chow was accurate but should not carry a halal guarantee. This pass keeps Durban curry in a hollowed loaf and adds beans as a common vegetarian option.
recommended_changes: Use a quarter or half loaf of white bread.
Fill with Durban-style lamb, chicken, bean, or vegetable curry.
Keep the scooped bread lid for dipping.

recipe.description: Durban bunny chow, a hollowed loaf filled with lamb, chicken, bean, or vegetable curry and served with the bread lid for scooping.
recipe.story: The bread is the bowl and the utensil. The curry should be thick enough to soak the walls without collapsing the loaf.
recipe.best_served_with: Carrot sambal, chopped onion, cucumber salad, achar, or extra gravy

replace_ingredients.name: lamb, chicken, beef, beans, or vegetables
unsliced white bread
replace_ingredients.prep_note: cut into quarters or halves and hollowed

sources.title: Penguin Random House South Africa - Bunny Chow PDF

chakalaka-za.official_source_status: South African references consistently describe chakalaka as a spicy vegetable relish with onion, tomato, carrot, peppers, beans, curry spice, and sometimes cabbage.
chakalaka-za.review_summary: Chakalaka was good. This pass removes halal as irrelevant and adds cabbage as an optional common vegetable while keeping baked beans, carrots, peppers, curry, and tomato.
recommended_changes: Cook onion, garlic, curry powder, carrots, peppers, tomato, and beans.
Add cabbage if desired.
Serve warm or room temperature with pap and braai.

recipe.description: South African chakalaka, a spicy vegetable relish with carrots, peppers, tomato, baked beans, curry powder, onion, garlic, and chile.
recipe.story: Chakalaka should be bright, spicy, and spoonable. Cook the vegetables until tender but not tired, then let the relish settle before serving.
recipe.best_served_with: Pap, braai meat, boerewors, bread, rice, or samp

asida.official_source_status: South Sudan sources describe asida as a cooked staple made from grains such as sorghum, millet, or corn and served with stew.
asida.review_summary: South Sudanese asida was incorrectly centered on wheat. This pass changes the base to sorghum, millet, or maize flour and removes halal and ghee from the core side-dish recipe.
recommended_changes: Use sorghum, millet, or maize flour.
Stir into boiling water until smooth and thick.
Shape into a dome with a well for stew.

recipe.description: South Sudanese asida, a smooth dome of sorghum, millet, or maize flour served with stew in the center.
recipe.story: Asida is about texture. Stir hard until it turns smooth and heavy, then shape it while hot so the stew has a place to pool.
recipe.best_served_with: Bamia, mulah, meat stew, okra sauce, or vegetable stew

replace_steps.title: Boil water
replace_steps.instruction: Bring water to a boil with salt and oil if using.

replace_steps.title: Stir flour
replace_steps.instruction: Lower heat and add flour gradually while stirring hard to prevent lumps.

replace_steps.title: Cook thick
replace_steps.instruction: Keep stirring until the asida is smooth, thick, and pulls from the pot.

replace_steps.title: Shape
replace_steps.instruction: Wet a bowl or spoon, shape into a dome, and make a well for stew.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Spoon bamia, mulah, or another stew into the well and around the base.

bamia.official_source_status: South Sudan and Sudan references consistently describe bamia as okra cooked with lamb or meat in tomato, onion, and garlic sauce, served with asida or kisra.
bamia.review_summary: Bamia was directionally correct but needed a longer lamb simmer and lemon or lime finish. This pass removes halal as a guarantee.
recommended_changes: Cook lamb until nearly tender before adding okra.
Use tomato, tomato paste, onion, garlic, coriander or cumin, and lemon or lime.
Serve with asida, kisra, or rice.

recipe.description: South Sudanese bamia, lamb and okra stew with tomato, onion, garlic, coriander, and a bright lemon finish.
recipe.story: Give the meat its time before the okra joins the pot. That keeps the lamb tender and the okra from disappearing.
recipe.best_served_with: Asida, kisra, rice, or flatbread

replace_ingredients.name: okra
replace_ingredients.prep_note: trimmed, left whole if small or sliced if large

replace_steps.title: Brown meat
replace_steps.instruction: Season and brown the meat in oil until the edges color.

replace_steps.title: Build tomato base
replace_steps.instruction: Add onion, garlic, tomatoes, tomato paste, coriander, and cumin if using. Cook until the tomatoes soften.

replace_steps.title: Simmer meat
replace_steps.instruction: Add water or stock and simmer until the meat is nearly tender.

replace_steps.title: Add okra
replace_steps.instruction: Add okra and cook until tender and the stew thickens.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in lemon or lime juice and adjust salt before serving.

kisra-mulah.official_source_status: Sudan and South Sudan references consistently describe kisra as fermented sorghum flatbread served with mullah or stew.
kisra-mulah.review_summary: Kisra with Mulah was missing the real fermentation time and incorrectly carried nut-free while peanut butter was optional. This pass makes the sorghum fermentation and stew timing honest.
recommended_changes: Ferment sorghum batter for at least eight hours.
Cook kisra thin and one-sided.
Serve with mulah, commonly okra or meat stew.

recipe.description: South Sudanese kisra with mulah, fermented sorghum flatbread served with a rich okra and meat stew.
recipe.story: Kisra needs fermentation for its tang and texture. Cook it thin, then use it like edible cloth for scooping the mulah.
recipe.best_served_with: Bamia, meat mulah, okra sauce, lentil stew, or vegetable stew

replace_steps.title: Ferment kisra
replace_steps.instruction: Mix sorghum flour and warm water into a thin batter. Cover and ferment until lightly sour and bubbly.

replace_steps.title: Cook mulah base
replace_steps.instruction: Brown meat in oil, then add onions, garlic, tomatoes, and water or stock. Simmer until the meat is tender.

replace_steps.title: Finish mulah
replace_steps.instruction: Add okra and peanut butter if using. Simmer until the stew thickens.

replace_steps.title: Cook kisra
replace_steps.instruction: Salt the fermented batter, thin if needed, and pour a very thin layer on a hot pan. Cook one side until set.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Fold or roll kisra and serve with mulah for scooping.

basbousa-sd.official_source_status: Sudanese and regional sources consistently describe basbousa as a semolina cake soaked in sugar syrup, often with yoghurt, butter, coconut, almonds, rose water, or orange blossom.
basbousa-sd.review_summary: Basbousa was broadly correct. This pass makes the semolina rest and syrup soak honest, keeps yoghurt, butter, almonds, rose water, and removes halal as a blanket tag.
recommended_changes: Rest the semolina batter before baking.
Pour cool syrup over hot cake.
Let the cake soak before serving.

recipe.description: Sudanese-style basbousa, a syrup-soaked semolina cake with yoghurt, butter, almonds, coconut, and rose water.
recipe.story: The syrup has to meet the cake at the right moment. Cold syrup over hot basbousa gives a tender, sweet crumb without making it soggy.
recipe.best_served_with: Tea, coffee, fresh fruit, or extra syrup

ful-medames-sd.official_source_status: Sudanese references consistently describe ful as slow-cooked fava beans served with sesame oil, lemon, cumin, onion, garlic, chile, and bread.
ful-medames-sd.review_summary: Sudanese ful was good but the page ignored overnight soaking. This pass makes the prep time honest and keeps sesame oil, lemon, cumin, onion, garlic, and chile.
recommended_changes: Soak dried fava beans overnight or use cooked beans.
Cook until very soft and mash roughly.
Finish Sudanese-style with sesame oil and lemon.

recipe.description: Sudanese ful medames, slow-cooked fava beans mashed with cumin, lemon, garlic, onion, chile, and sesame oil.
recipe.story: Ful should be creamy but never lifeless. Keep a little texture in the beans and let the sesame oil finish the bowl.
recipe.best_served_with: Warm bread, boiled eggs, salad, feta, pickles, or extra lemon

kisra-mullah-sd.official_source_status: Sudanese references describe kisra as fermented sorghum flatbread and mullah tagalia as onion, tomato paste, minced or dried beef, oil, and spices cooked thick.
kisra-mullah-sd.review_summary: Kisra with mullah tagalia needed fermentation time and a more accurate tagalia description. This pass centers onions, tomato paste, minced or dried beef, oil, coriander, and red pepper.
recommended_changes: Ferment kisra batter for 12 to 24 hours.
Cook tagalia until onions darken and oil rises.
Remove halal and add gluten-free because the batter is sorghum-led.

recipe.description: Sudanese kisra with mullah tagalia, fermented sorghum crepes served with thick onion, tomato, meat, coriander, and red pepper sauce.
recipe.story: Good kisra brings the tang, and tagalia brings the depth. Cook the onions long enough for the sauce to turn dark and glossy.
recipe.best_served_with: Salad, lime, extra chile, or asida

chipsi-mayai.official_source_status: Tanzanian references consistently describe chipsi mayai as a street-food omelette made with French fries and eggs.
chipsi-mayai.review_summary: Chipsi mayai was accurate. This pass removes halal as irrelevant, keeps the fry-and-omelette method, and names kachumbari/pili pili as the table.
recommended_changes: Fry chips first.
Pour seasoned eggs over chips in the pan.
Serve with kachumbari, ketchup, or pili pili.

recipe.description: Tanzanian chipsi mayai, a street-food omelette built around crisp fries, eggs, onion, tomato, and chile.
recipe.story: The chips have to stay proud inside the egg. Fry them first, then set the omelette around them instead of drowning them.
recipe.best_served_with: Kachumbari, ketchup, pili pili sauce, or extra tomato salad

tanzanian-pilau.official_source_status: Tanzanian and Zanzibari sources consistently describe pilau as spiced rice with onions, whole spices, meat or potatoes, garlic, ginger, and stock.
tanzanian-pilau.review_summary: Tanzanian pilau was strong. This pass removes halal guarantee, keeps whole spice depth, and adds ginger as a common coastal note.
recommended_changes: Caramelize onions well.
Use whole spices with garlic and ginger.
Cook rice in meat or vegetable stock until dry and fluffy.

recipe.description: Tanzanian pilau, Zanzibar-influenced spiced rice with caramelized onions, meat, potatoes, garlic, ginger, and whole spices.
recipe.story: Pilau lives in the onion and spice oil. Let the onions color deeply before rice and stock go in.
recipe.best_served_with: Kachumbari, banana, pili pili, coconut beans, or grilled meat

ugali-na-nyama.official_source_status: Tanzanian references consistently describe ugali as a maize staple eaten with meat, fish, beans, or vegetable stews.
ugali-na-nyama.review_summary: Ugali na nyama was fine structurally. This pass removes halal guarantee and makes beef stew timing a bit more realistic.
recommended_changes: Cook beef stew until tender before making ugali.
Use maize flour and water for a firm ugali.
Serve immediately while both are hot.

recipe.description: Tanzanian ugali na nyama, firm maize ugali served with slow-simmered beef, tomato, onion, garlic, and pepper stew.
recipe.story: Start the stew first. Ugali takes minutes, but nyama needs time to soften and make sauce worth scooping.
recipe.best_served_with: Kachumbari, sukuma wiki, pili pili, or avocado

akoume-tg.official_source_status: Togolese references consistently describe akoumé as a firm corn-flour dough served with sauces such as gboma dessi.
akoume-tg.review_summary: Akoumé was accurate. This pass keeps corn dough, slurry-first cooking, and pairing with sauces, while tightening naming and story.
recommended_changes: Use corn flour or a corn-flour blend.
Start with slurry to prevent lumps.
Stir until thick enough to mound.

recipe.description: Togolese akoumé, a firm corn-flour dough shaped into a smooth mound and served with rich sauces.
recipe.story: The trick is smoothness. Start with a slurry, then add flour gradually and stir until the dough holds its shape.
recipe.best_served_with: Gboma dessi, tomato sauce, okra sauce, grilled fish, or chicken stew

fufu-gboma-dessi-tg.official_source_status: Togolese references describe gboma dessi as a leafy tomato sauce served with akoumé or fufu and commonly cooked with meat, shrimp, crab, or smoked fish.
fufu-gboma-dessi-tg.review_summary: Gboma dessi was too seafood-specific for a dish that commonly uses meat, shrimp, crab, or smoked fish. This pass adds garlic, ginger, chile, tomato sauce, and longer stew time.
recommended_changes: Use spinach or gboma greens with tomato, onion, garlic, ginger, palm oil, and chile.
Allow beef, crab, shrimp, or smoked fish as common proteins.
Simmer longer so greens and sauce integrate.

recipe.description: Togolese fufu with gboma dessi, pounded yam served with spinach tomato stew, palm oil, garlic, ginger, chile, and seafood or meat.
recipe.story: Gboma dessi needs the greens to cook into the tomato base. Let the sauce thicken until it clings to the fufu.
recipe.best_served_with: Akoumé, ablo, rice, or extra chile sauce

replace_ingredients.name: beef, smoked fish, crab, or shrimp
red palm oil or vegetable oil

koklo-meme-tg.official_source_status: Togolese references describe koklo meme as grilled chicken marinated with ginger, garlic, chile, citrus, and spices.
koklo-meme-tg.review_summary: Koklo meme was close, but prep time ignored the marinade. This pass increases prep time and removes halal guarantee.
recommended_changes: Marinate chicken with ginger, garlic, chile, citrus, oil, salt, and pepper.
Grill over charcoal.
Rest before cutting.

recipe.description: Togolese koklo meme, charcoal-grilled chicken marinated with ginger, garlic, chile, lime, oil, salt, and pepper.
recipe.story: The marinade should get under the skin and into the cuts. Let the chicken sit, then grill until the edges char.
recipe.best_served_with: Akoumé, rice, salad, piment sauce, or fried plantains

brik-tn.official_source_status: Tunisian sources consistently describe brik à l'oeuf as thin pastry filled with egg, tuna, capers, parsley, and onion, then fried crisp.
brik-tn.review_summary: Brik was accurate. This pass removes halal guarantee and keeps malsouka, egg, tuna, capers, parsley, onion, lemon, and harissa.
recommended_changes: Use malsouka or brik pastry.
Keep the egg yolk soft if possible.
Serve immediately with lemon and harissa.

recipe.description: Tunisian brik à l'oeuf, a crisp malsouka pastry filled with egg, tuna, capers, parsley, onion, lemon, and harissa.
recipe.story: Brik waits for no one. Assemble, fry, drain briefly, and eat while the shell still shatters.
recipe.best_served_with: Lemon wedges, harissa, salad mechouia, or olives

lablabi-tn.official_source_status: Tunisian sources consistently describe lablabi as chickpea broth with garlic and cumin poured over stale bread, finished with harissa, olive oil, lemon, capers, and optional egg or tuna.
lablabi-tn.review_summary: Lablabi was accurate but ignored overnight chickpea soaking. This pass makes the timer honest and removes halal as irrelevant.
recommended_changes: Soak dried chickpeas overnight.
Cook chickpeas in garlic-cumin broth.
Serve over stale bread with harissa, olive oil, lemon, capers, tuna, or egg as options.

recipe.description: Tunisian lablabi, garlic-cumin chickpea broth ladled over torn bread with harissa, olive oil, lemon, capers, and optional egg or tuna.
recipe.story: The bowl is assembled, not just served. Bread first, hot chickpeas next, then each person tunes the heat, oil, and lemon.
recipe.best_served_with: Harissa, lemon, capers, tuna, soft egg, olives, or extra olive oil

ojja-tn.official_source_status: Tunisian sources consistently describe ojja as a tomato, pepper, harissa, garlic, and egg skillet, often with merguez or seafood.
ojja-tn.review_summary: Ojja was accurate but diet tags over-promised. This pass removes halal and gluten-free, and keeps merguez, harissa, tomato, peppers, caraway, coriander, and eggs.
recommended_changes: Brown merguez first.
Build a tomato-pepper-harissa sauce with garlic and spices.
Poach eggs gently in the sauce.

recipe.description: Tunisian ojja with merguez, eggs poached in a fiery tomato, pepper, harissa, garlic, caraway, and coriander sauce.
recipe.story: Ojja is bolder than a gentle egg breakfast. Let the harissa bloom in oil before the eggs go in.
recipe.best_served_with: Crusty bread, olives, salad, or extra harissa

sources.title: Le Creuset - Tunisian Ojja with Merguez

luwombo.official_source_status: Ugandan references consistently describe luwombo as chicken, beef, mushrooms, fish, or groundnut sauce wrapped in banana leaves and steamed.
luwombo.review_summary: Luwombo was accurate but diet tags over-promised. This pass removes halal and keeps banana-leaf steaming with chicken and groundnut sauce.
recommended_changes: Soften banana leaves before wrapping.
Wrap chicken, vegetables, and groundnut sauce tightly.
Steam long enough for the sauce to thicken inside the parcel.

recipe.description: Ugandan luwombo, chicken, beef, fish, mushrooms, or groundnut sauce wrapped in banana leaves and steamed until tender.
recipe.story: The banana leaf is the pot. Seal it well and let the steam build a sauce inside the parcel.
recipe.best_served_with: Matoke, rice, posho, sweet potatoes, or greens

matoke.official_source_status: Ugandan references consistently describe matoke as green cooking bananas steamed, mashed, and served with sauce, beans, groundnut sauce, or meat.
matoke.review_summary: Matoke was accurate. This pass removes halal as irrelevant and keeps the steamed banana-leaf method with savory tomato-onion sauce.
recommended_changes: Use green cooking bananas.
Steam wrapped in banana leaves if available.
Mash with tomato-onion sauce and serve with groundnut sauce, beans, or meat.

recipe.description: Ugandan matoke, steamed green cooking bananas mashed into tomato, onion, garlic, and pepper sauce.
recipe.story: The bananas should steam until fully soft before mashing. That is how matoke becomes smooth instead of chunky.
recipe.best_served_with: Groundnut sauce, beans, beef stew, chicken luwombo, greens, or avocado

rolex-uganda.official_source_status: Ugandan references consistently describe rolex as a chapati rolled around eggs and vegetables, sold as street food.
rolex-uganda.review_summary: Rolex was accurate. This pass removes halal, keeps chapati plus vegetable omelette, and adds a slightly longer chapati rest.
recommended_changes: Cook chapati first.
Make a thin vegetable omelette.
Roll the two together tightly while hot.

recipe.description: Ugandan rolex, a hot chapati rolled around an egg omelette with onion, tomato, cabbage, and pepper.
recipe.story: The roll has to happen while both chapati and egg are hot, so the layers bend together instead of cracking.
recipe.best_served_with: Tea, kachumbari, avocado, hot sauce, or extra cabbage

sahrawi-camel-stew-eh.official_source_status: Direct Sahrawi camel stew recipes are scarce. Western Saharan food references consistently identify camel as a favored meat and mention meat stews with onion, garlic, and bread.
sahrawi-camel-stew-eh.review_summary: The Sahrawi camel stew was plausible but needed garlic, longer cooking, and no halal guarantee. This pass keeps the desert camel, onion, tomato, and communal bread context.
recommended_changes: Cook camel low and slow until tender.
Add garlic alongside onion and tomato.
Serve with flatbread and tea.

recipe.description: Sahrawi-style camel meat stew with onions, garlic, tomatoes, potatoes, cumin, coriander, and slow desert cooking.
recipe.story: Camel needs patience. Brown it first, then let the pot simmer until the meat relaxes into the tomato-onion sauce.
recipe.best_served_with: Flatbread, couscous, dates, or Sahrawi mint tea

sahrawi-mint-tea-eh.official_source_status: Sahrawi and Maghrebi tea references consistently describe green tea with mint and sugar, poured from height and served in repeated rounds.
sahrawi-mint-tea-eh.review_summary: Sahrawi mint tea was accurate. This pass removes halal as irrelevant and keeps the three-round foam ritual.
recommended_changes: Use gunpowder green tea, sugar, and mint.
Pour from height to build foam.
Serve in three rounds, getting milder and sweeter.

recipe.description: Sahrawi mint tea, strong gunpowder green tea with fresh mint and sugar, poured from height and served in three rounds.
recipe.story: Foam matters. Pour high, return the tea to the pot, and repeat until the glasses carry a pale frothy crown.
recipe.best_served_with: Dates, nuts, flatbread, camel stew, or couscous

sources.title: How to Brew Sahrawi Tea the Traditional Way

sahrawi-couscous-eh.official_source_status: Western Saharan references identify couscous as a staple served with meat and vegetables; precise household vegetable combinations vary.
sahrawi-couscous-eh.review_summary: The couscous was plausible and well structured. This pass removes halal as irrelevant and adds chickpeas or raisins as optional desert pantry notes.
recommended_changes: Steam couscous in stages.
Use onions, carrots, turnips, squash, and light broth.
Add chickpeas or raisins if available.

recipe.description: Sahrawi vegetable couscous steamed with onion, carrot, turnip, squash, chickpeas, olive oil, and light broth.
recipe.story: The couscous should be steamed and fluffed, not soaked into paste. Treat the grains gently and let the vegetables season the broth.
recipe.best_served_with: Camel stew, goat, leben, harissa, or mint tea

chikanda.official_source_status: Zambian references consistently describe chikanda as a savory cake made from wild orchid tubers and peanuts, cooked, set, and sliced.
chikanda.review_summary: Chikanda was accurate but the timer ignored the chilling stage and halal was irrelevant. This pass makes the set time visible.
recommended_changes: Use chikanda orchid powder, ground peanuts, baking soda, chile, and salt.
Cook while stirring until thick.
Chill until firm before slicing.

recipe.description: Zambian chikanda, African polony made from wild orchid tuber powder, ground peanuts, baking soda, chile, and salt.
recipe.story: Chikanda has to set before it slices cleanly. Cook it thick, press it firmly, then give it time to chill.
recipe.best_served_with: Nshima, tea, tomato relish, chile sauce, or salad

kapenta.official_source_status: Zambian references consistently describe kapenta as small dried fish fried with tomato and onion and eaten with nshima.
kapenta.review_summary: Kapenta was accurate. This pass removes halal and keeps the rinse, fry, tomato-onion relish, and nshima pairing.
recommended_changes: Rinse dried kapenta well.
Fry before adding tomatoes and onions.
Serve with nshima.

recipe.description: Zambian kapenta, tiny dried fish rinsed, fried crisp, then cooked with onion, tomato, garlic, green pepper, and chile.
recipe.story: Fry the kapenta first so it stays savory and firm before the tomato relish wraps around it.
recipe.best_served_with: Nshima, rice, ifisashi, tomato relish, or greens

nshima-ifisashi.official_source_status: Zambian references consistently describe ifisashi as greens cooked in peanut sauce and served with nshima.
nshima-ifisashi.review_summary: Nshima with Ifisashi was accurate. This pass removes halal and adds more flexible greens.
recommended_changes: Use maize meal for nshima.
Cook greens in peanut sauce with tomato and onion.
Use rape, pumpkin leaves, cassava leaves, spinach, or other greens.

recipe.description: Zambian nshima with ifisashi, firm maize porridge served with leafy greens cooked in tomato-onion peanut sauce.
recipe.story: The ifisashi should be creamy but not heavy. Let the greens soften before the peanut sauce thickens around them.
recipe.best_served_with: Kapenta, beans, chicken, tomato relish, or chile

replace_ingredients.name: rape leaves, pumpkin leaves, spinach, or cassava leaves

dovi-zw.official_source_status: Zimbabwean dovi references consistently describe chicken or vegetables stewed with peanut butter, tomato, onion, garlic, pepper, and greens.
dovi-zw.review_summary: Dovi was sound but needed garlic, ginger, and pepper in the ingredient list. This pass keeps chicken, peanut butter, tomato, greens, and sadza service.
recommended_changes: Brown chicken first.
Cook onion, garlic, ginger, tomato, pepper, and peanut butter into a sauce.
Add greens near the end.

recipe.description: Zimbabwean dovi, chicken stewed with peanut butter, tomatoes, onion, garlic, ginger, pepper, and pumpkin leaves or spinach.
recipe.story: Dovi is gentle comfort, but the peanut must cook through. Simmer until the sauce is creamy and no longer raw-tasting.
recipe.best_served_with: Sadza, rice, muriwo, roasted vegetables, or fresh tomato salad

madora.official_source_status: Zimbabwean references consistently identify madora or mopane worms as a common protein eaten dried, fried, and often cooked with tomato and onion.
madora.review_summary: Madora was plausible. This pass removes halal and keeps the soaking, frying, tomato-onion sauce, and optional peanuts.
recommended_changes: Soak dried mopane worms until flexible.
Fry to firm the texture.
Finish with onion, tomato, garlic, chile, and optional crushed peanuts.

recipe.description: Zimbabwean madora, dried mopane worms soaked, fried crisp, and cooked with onion, tomato, garlic, chile, and optional peanuts.
recipe.story: The soak softens them; the fry gives them bite. Finish in tomato relish so the flavor sits comfortably with sadza.
recipe.best_served_with: Sadza, muriwo, tomato relish, roasted peanuts, or rice

mapopo-candy-zw.official_source_status: Zimbabwe recipe references describe mapopo candy as green papaya cooked in sugar syrup, often with lemon, mint, vanilla, or color, then dried.
mapopo-candy-zw.review_summary: Mapopo candy was accurate but the timer ignored drying. This pass adds drying/resting time to the total.
recommended_changes: Use green papaya.
Cook slowly in syrup until translucent.
Dry the candy before storing.

recipe.description: Zimbabwean mapopo candy, green papaya simmered in sugar syrup with lemon, then dried until jewel-like and lightly crystallized.
recipe.story: The syrup makes it translucent, but the drying makes it candy. Give the pieces time on a rack before storing.
recipe.best_served_with: Tea, coffee, fresh fruit, or as a small sweet after sadza
